questions.question: The point of intersection of X and Y axes is called
questions.explanation:
In two dimensions, On the flat coordinate plane, there are two axes,
the vertical y-axis and the horizontal x-axis.
The origin is the point where they intersect.
This point has the coordinates (0,0) and is usually labelled with the letter O.

questions.question: The distance of the point (-3, -2) from x-axis is
questions.explanation: Distance from the x-axis is equal to the y-coordinate of a given point (-3, -2), and distance is taken as positive, so the distance from x-axis = 2 units.

questions.question: The distance of the point (-6, -2) from y-axis is
questions.explanation: Distance from y-axis is equal to the x-cordinate of given point(-6, -2) so, distance from y-axis = 6units [since distance is taken as positve]

questions.question: The abscissa and ordinate of the point with Co-ordinates (8, 12) is
questions.explanation:
In the Cartesian plane, any point P is written as p(x, y),
where X co-ordinate is called the abscissa of point p
and Y co-ordinate is called ordinate of point p.
So, here abscissa will be equal to 8 and ordinate = 12

questions.question: The co-ordinate of origin is
questions.explanation:
The center of the coordinate system (where the lines intersect) is called the origin.
The axes intersect when both x and y are zero.
The coordinates of the origin are (0, 0).

questions.question: The distance of the point (2,3) from the y-axis
questions.explanation: The distance from y-axis is equal to the x-coordinate, so distance = 2 units

questions.question: The point (-2, -1) lies in
questions.explanation: 3rd quadrant because co-ordinate of point in 3rd quadrant is (-x,-y)

questions.question: The point (3, 0) lies on
questions.explanation:
Any point in co-ordinate is written as P(x, y) 
So, when the value of y becomes zero.
i.e, y = 0 then the point p lies on the x-axis.
So, here the point will lie on +ve direction of the x-axis.

questions.question: The distance of the point (3, 5) from x- axis is
questions.explanation:
Distance of any point from x-axis is y-coordinate of that given point

So ,distance =5 unit

questions.question: The point (-2, 5) lies in
questions.explanation: For any point to lie in the second quadrant, Abscissa and ordinate must be (-, +). So the given point (-2, 5) lies in 2nd quadrant.

questions.question: The distance of the point (0, 3) from x-axis is
questions.explanation:
The y-coordinate of given point is the distance of any point from x-axis.
So, distance = 3 unit

questions.question: The point A(3, 4) lies in
questions.explanation:
In 1st quadrant sign of both coordinates is positive i.e, (+, +)
So, (3,4) will lie in quadrant I.

questions.question: The point B(-3, 4) lies in
questions.explanation:
In 2nd quadrant, Abscissa is (—ve)and Ordinate is (+ve) , i.e, (-x , y)

And here value of x is negative and y is positive, so it will lies in 2nd quadrant

questions.question: The point C(-5, -2) lies in
questions.explanation:
In 3rd quadrant values of Abscissa and Ordinate both are —ve , i.e, (— , +)

Here,since value of both x and y-coordinate are -ve so it will lies in 3rd quadrant

questions.question: The point D(3, -6) lies in
questions.explanation:
In 4th quadrant values of Abscissa is (+) and Ordinate is (-) 
In this question value of x-coordinate is +ve and y-coordinate is -ve, so it will lie on 4th quadrant.

questions.question: The point at which the two co-ordinate axes meet is called the
questions.explanation: In co-ordinate system, there are two axes; x-axis and y-axis, point of intersection of x-axis and y-axis is called the origin.

questions.question: The points (other than the origin) for which the abscissa is equal to the ordinate lie in
questions.explanation:
We have to take those points where abscissa and ordinate are equal.
Two cases arise;
i) (2, 2) = since both values are +ve so it will lie in 1st quadrant.
ii) (-2, -2) = since both values are -ve so it will lie in 3rd quadrant.

questions.question: A point both of whose coordinates are negative will lie in
questions.explanation: In 3rd quadrant sign of both x and y-coordinate are negative, i.e,(-x, -y)

questions.question: Abscissa of a point is positive in:
questions.explanation:
Abscissa means x-corrdinate.
In quadrant I and IV, the value of x is positive. 
Thus, the abscissa of a point is positive in quadrant I and IV.

questions.question: Abscissa of a point is negative in
questions.explanation:
The abscissa (x-axis) is -ve in 2nd and 3rd quadrant only because,
Sign of point in 2nd quadrant is (-, +), and in 3rd quadrant, it is (-, -).

questions.question: Ordinate of a point is positive in
questions.explanation:
Since,sign of point in 1st quadrant is (+,+),

And in second quadrant it is (—,+)

So,Ordinat of a point is +ve only in 1st and 2nd quadrant

questions.question: Ordinate of a point is negative in
questions.explanation:
Since, sign of point in 3rd quadrant is (-, -).
And in 4th quadrant, it is (+, -).
So, Ordinate of a point is -ve only in 3rd and 4th quadrant.

questions.question: Which of the following are the signs of abscissa and ordinate of a point in quadrant I?
questions.explanation: The signs of abscissa and ordinate of a point in quadrant I are both +ve i.e. (+, +)

questions.question: Signs of the abscissa and ordinate of a point in the second quadrant are respectively.
questions.explanation:
Abscissa means just the horizontal axis i.e. x axis. Ordinate means just the vertical axis i.e. y axis.
x-corrdinate and y-corrdinate make a point.
The signs of abscissa and ordinate of a point in quadrant II are (-, +).

questions.question: The signs of abscissa and ordinate of a point in quadrant III are
questions.explanation:
The signs of abscissa and ordinate of a point in quadrant III are both —ve, 

i.e, in quadrant III co-ordinates Of point is (—,—).

questions.question: The signs of abscissa and ordinate of a point in quadrant IV are
questions.explanation:
In the Cartesian plane, if co-ordinate of any point P is P(x, y), then x-coordinate is called the abscissa of the point and, y-coordinate is called ordinate of the point.
The signs of abscissa and ordinate of a point in quadrant IV is (+, -)

questions.question: If A(2, 3) and B(-3, 4), then (abscissa of A) – (abscissa of B) is
questions.explanation:
Here we have, the abscissa of A = 2 and abscissa of B = -3.
So, according to question,
(abscissa of A) - (abscissa of B)
= 2 - (-3)
= 5

questions.question: If P(3, 9) and Q(-3, -4), then (abscissa of P) - (ordinate of Q) is
questions.explanation:
From the given data we have,
The abscissa of P = 3 and ordinate of Q = -4,
So, according to question,
(abscissa of P) - (ordinate of Q) 
= 3 - (-4)
= 7

questions.question: A point of the form (a, 0) lies on
questions.explanation:
The given point of the form = (a, 0)
Here, x-co-ordinate = a and y-co-ordinate = 0
∴ The point of the form (a, 0) always lies on x-axis.
Thus, the point of the form (a, 0) always lies on x-axis.

questions.question: A point of the form {tex}(0,b){/tex} lies on:
questions.explanation:
Let {tex}P{/tex} be any point whose co-ordinate be {tex}P(0,b){/tex}

Then, if the value of x-coordinate or abscissa is zero then the point {tex}P{/tex} lies in y-axis.

questions.question: The co-ordinates of the origin are
questions.explanation:
In co-ordinate, there are two Axis one is x-axis and other is y-axis, the point of intersection of both x-axis and y-axis is origin.
Coordinate of origin is always (0, 0).

questions.question: The equation of x-axis is
questions.explanation:
In co-ordinate, any point P is written as p(x, y).
When Y = 0 then the point P lies on x-axis.
So, Y = 0 is the equation of the x-axis.

questions.question: The equation of y-axis is:
questions.explanation:
The value of abscissa or x-corrdinate is always zero at any point on y-axis.

So, x = 0 is the equation of y-axis.

questions.question: A(-6, 3) be a point on the graph. Draw {tex}AL \bot x - axis{/tex}. The co-ordinates of L are
questions.explanation:
Since AL perpendicular to x-axis,

So ,point L lies on x-axis, and we know that for any point on x-axis y-ordinate is zero.

So,we have L= (—6 , 0 )

questions.question: P(5, -7) be a point on the graph. Draw the PM {tex}\perp{/tex} y-axis. The coordinates of M are
questions.explanation:
Here, PM Perpendicular to y-axis.
So point M lies on the y-axis, and for any point on y-axis always the value of x = 0.
So Co-ordinate of M = (0, -7).

questions.question: Which point does not lie in any quadrant?
questions.explanation: Since here value of x-coordinate = 0. So point lies on the y-axis and not in any quadrant.

questions.question: The point O (0, 0) lies on:
questions.explanation: Point (0, 0) is the co-ordinate of origin and origin is the point of intersection of x and y-axis. So, point O (0, 0) lies on both axis.

questions.question: The perpendicular distance of the point P(-2, -3) from the y-axis is
questions.explanation:
Perpendicular distance of any point from y-axis is the given x-coordinate of point,

So distance=2unit

questions.question: The perpendicular distance of the point P(-3, -6) from the x-axis is
questions.explanation:
Perpendicular distance of a point from x-axis is y-ordinate of the given point is (-3, - 6).
Distance = 6 unit

questions.question: The name of the horizontal line drawn to determine the position of any point in the Cartesian plane is
questions.explanation:
In co-ordinate, we have two axis, one is a horizontal line called x-axis and the other is vertical line called y-axis.
Used to determine the position of any point in Cartesian plane.

questions.question: The name of the vertical line drawn to determine the position of any point in the Cartesian plane is
questions.explanation: In the Cartesian plane, there are two axes. One is a horizontal line, that is called the x-axis and other is a vertical line, that is called y-axis.

questions.question: The name of each part of the plane formed by the two lines in the Cartesian plane is
questions.explanation:
In the Cartesian plane, there are two Axis one is x axis and other is y axis.

when the both Axis cut one another it forms four quadrant( quadrant 1,2,3and 4)

questions.question: The point (-3, 0) lies
questions.explanation:
Since value of y-ordinate is zero, so point lies on x-axis.
But value of x is -ve so, it lies on negative direction of x-axis.

questions.question: The point (7, 0) lies
questions.explanation:
Since value of y-ordinate is zero so, point lies on x-axis.
But value of x is +ve so it lies on +ve direction of x-axis.

questions.question: The point (0, -4) lies
questions.explanation:
Since x = 0 so point lies on y-axis, but value of y is -ve.
So, point lies on -ve direction of y-axis.

questions.question: The point (0, 9) lies
questions.explanation:
Any point P in co-ordinate plane is written as P(x,y)

when the value of x-coordinate is equal to zero then the point P lies on y axis

Since,here x=0 so,point lies on y-axis 

And the value of y is positive so,

Points lies in the positive direction of       y-axis

questions.question: Abscissa of all points on the x-axis is
questions.explanation: Abscissa of point in x axis is can be any number but ordinate will always be zero, because for any point to lie on x-axis its y-ordinate must be equal to zero.

questions.question: Abscissa of all the points on the y-axis is:
questions.explanation:
On y-axis, x-coordinate i.e. abscissa is 0.
Thus, abscissa of all the points on y-axis is 0.

questions.question: Ordinate of all points on the x-axis is
questions.explanation:
In cartesian plane any point P is written as P(x, y) when the value of Y co-ordinate is equal to zero then the point P lies on x-axis.
So, for any point on x-axis, ordinate = 0

questions.question: Ordinate of all points on the y-axis is
questions.explanation:
In the cartesian plane any point P is written as p(x, y)

when the value of x co-ordinate is equal to zero then the point P lies on y axis,

So,Ordinate of any point on y-axis can be any number but abscissa will be zero

questions.question: Points (2, -3), (4, -5), (5, -9) and (-2, -5)
questions.explanation:
Point (2, -3),(4, -5),(5, -9) lies in 4th quadrant, because sign of abscissa and ordinate in 4th quadrant is (+, -).
But point ( -2, -5) lies in 3rd quadrant since,sign of abscissa and ordinate in 3rd quadrant is ( -, -).

questions.question: If O(0, 0), A(3, 0), B(3, 4) and C(0, 4) are the vertices of a quadrilateral OABC, then OABC is:
questions.explanation:
As O(0, 0) and A(3, 0)
So, Length of side OA = 3 units
As A(3, 0) and B(3, 4)
So, Length of side AB = 4 units
As O(0, 0) and C(0, 4)
So, Length of side OC = 4 units
As B(3, 4) and C(0, 4)
So, Length of side BC = 3 units
As O(0, 0) and B(3, 4)
So, Length of diagonal OB = 5 units
As A(3, 0) and C(0, 4)
So, Length of diagonal AC = 5 units
In a quadrilateral opposite sides are equal and diagonals are also equal then it is a rectangle.
Therefore, OABC is a rectangle.

questions.question: If the y co-ordinate of a point is zero, then this point always lies:
questions.explanation: Every point on the x-axis is of the form (a, 0). This means abscissa can be any real number but ordinate is always 0.

questions.question: If the x co-ordinate of a point is zero, then this point always lies:
questions.explanation: If the x co-ordinate of a point is zero, then this point always lies on y-axis.

questions.question: The point whose ordinate is 6 and which point lies on the y-axis?
questions.explanation:
Since the ordinate or y-coordinate of a point is 6 and this point lies on y-axis.
And the abscissa or x-coordinate of a point lying on y-axis is 0.
Therefore, the coordinate of the point is (0, 6).

questions.question: The point whose abscissa is 4 and this point lies on the x-axis is:
questions.explanation:
Since the abscissa or x-coordinate of a point is 4 and this point lies on the x-axis. And the ordinate or y-coordinate of a point lying on the x-axis is 0.
Therefore the coordinate of the point is (4, 0).

questions.question: If P(5, 1), Q(8, 0), R(0, 4), S(0, 5) and O(0, 0) are plotted on the graph paper, then the points on the x-axis are
questions.explanation:
Co-ordinate of any point on x-axis is (x ,0)

And, we have point O and point Q with such co-ordinate so point O and Q will lie on x-axis.

questions.question: The point which lies on y-axis at a distance of 3 units in the negative direction of y-axis is
questions.explanation:
Since, it lies 3 units in the negative direction of the y-axis so its ordinate will be -3 and it also lies on the y-axis so, it's abscissa will be zero, because x-coordinate of any point on y-axis is zero.
Thus, point will be (0, -3).

questions.question: The point which lies on y-axis at a distance of 6 units in the positive direction of y-axis is
questions.explanation:
Since it lies on the y-axis so it's abscissa x will be zero.
Thus, the point will be (0, 6).

questions.question: The point which lies on x-axis at a distance of 4 units in the negative direction of x-axis is
questions.explanation:
Since it lies on x-axis so,ordinate will be zero,

Thus point will be (—4,0)

questions.question: The point which lies on x-axis at a distance of 3 units in the positive direction of x-axis is
questions.explanation:
Since it lies on x-axis so ordinate will be zero because the value of the y-coordinate in the x-axis is equal to zero.
Thus point will be (3, 0).

questions.question: The points A(-2, 3), B(-2, -4) and C(5, -4) are the vertices of the square ABCD, then the co-ordinates of the vertex D are:
questions.explanation:
Let A(-2, 3), B(-2, -4), C(5, -4) be the three vertices of the square ABCD.
Clearly, the abscissa of D = abscissa of C = 5
And,ordinate of D = ordinate of A = 3
So, the coordinates of the 4th vertex of ABCD i.e. D are (5, 3).

questions.question: If O(0, 0), A(4, 0) and B(0, 5) are the vertices of a triangle, then a triangle OAB is:
questions.explanation:
Since we have a point O(0, 0) i.e. origin.
Point A(4, 0) whose y-coordinate is zero. So, this point is on x-axis.
Point B(0, 5) whose x-coordinate is zero. So, this point is on y-axis.
Angle between x-axis (OA) and y-axis (OB) is 90 degrees. Thus, it forms a right triangle.

questions.question: If (x, y) = (y, x), then
questions.explanation:
If (x,y) = (y,x),

It means abscissa =ordinate or, x=y

So,

X—Y = 0 {since x=y,}

questions.question: x co-ordinate is known as
questions.explanation:
Any point p in cartesian plane is written as p(x, y).
x coordinate of point p is called abscissa and Y co-ordinate of point p is called ordinate.

questions.question: y co-ordinate is known as
questions.explanation:
Any point p in cartesian plane is written as p(x, y).
x coordinate of point p is called abscissa and Y co-ordinate of point p is called ordinate.

questions.question: The radius of a circle whose radius is 5 units and whose centre lies on the origin. The co-ordinates of any point lies on the circle and on the y-axis are
questions.explanation:
Since point lies on the y-axis so it can lie in two-point one in positive direction and another is negative direction of y-axis.
So point will be (0, 5) or (0, -5).

questions.question: The circle whose radius is 3 units and whose centre lies on the origin. The co-ordinates of any point lies on the circle and on the x-axis are
questions.explanation:
It can lie on positive or negative direction of x-axis,

So point will be (3,0) or (—3,0)

questions.question: A point is at a distance of 3 units from the x-axis and 7 units from the y-axis. Which of the following may be the co-ordinates of the point?
questions.explanation:
We know that distance of any point from x-axis is the y-ordinate, so here y-coordinate = 3.
Now, distance of any point from y-axis is the x coordinate of the point.
So, here x co-ordinate is = 7
Thus, point will be (7, 3)

questions.question: The co-ordinates of two points A and B are (4, 3) and (4, -5) respectively. The co-ordinates of the point at which the line segment AB meets the x-axis are
questions.explanation:
Since it will meet at x-axis and we know that any point on x-axis have ordinate equals to zero.
So point will be (4, 0).

questions.question: The co-ordinates of two points A and B are (4, 3) and (-5, 3) respectively. The co-ordinates of the point at which the line segment AB meets the y-axis are
questions.explanation: Since it meets at y-axis, so , abscissa will be zero and we have ordinate=3 in common so,point will be (0,3)

questions.question: The co-ordinates of a point below the x-axis lying on y-axis at a distance of 4 units are
questions.explanation:
Since, it lies on the y-axis so abscissa = 0
And since it lies 4 unit below x-axis so the value of ordinate = -4.
Thus, point will be (0, -4).

questions.question: The co-ordinates of a point above the x-axis lying on y-axis at a distance of 4 units are
questions.explanation:
It lies on y-axis so it's abscissa = 0 and it lies on y-axis at a distance of 4unit.
Thus point will be (0, 4).

questions.question: The distance of the point (-3, 4) from the origin is
questions.explanation:
Let the given point be (x1, y1) = (-3, 4) and the origin is (x2, y2) = (0, 0)
{tex}\therefore {/tex} The distance of the given point from the origin = {tex}\sqrt {{{\left( {{x_2} - {x_1}} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {{y_2} - {y_1}} \right)}^2}} {/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt {{{\left( {0 + 3} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {0 - 4} \right)}^2}} {/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt {9 + 16}{/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt {25} {/tex} = 5 units

questions.question: The distance between the points (-1, -5) and (-6, 7) is
questions.explanation:
Let point A be (x1, y1) = (-1, -5) and point B (x2, y2) = (-6, 7)
{tex}\therefore {/tex} Distance between A and B  
Using Distance Formula
= {tex}\sqrt {(x_2-x_1)^2 + (y_2-y_1)^2}{/tex}

= {tex}\sqrt {(-6+1)^2 + (7+5)^2}{/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt {25 + 144}{/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt {169} {/tex} = 13 units

questions.question: The distance between the points (a, b) and (-a, b) is
questions.explanation:
Let point A be (a, b) and point B is (-a, b)
{tex}\therefore {/tex} distance between A and B = {tex}\sqrt {{{\left( { - a - a} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {b - b} \right)}^2}} {/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt {4{a^2} + 0} {/tex}
= 2a units

questions.question: If the distance between the points (p, -5) and (2, 7) is 13 units, then the value of p is
questions.explanation:
Let point A be (p, -5) and point B (2, 7) and distance between A and B = 13 units
{tex}\therefore {/tex} 13 = {tex}\sqrt {{{\left( {2 - p} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {7 + 5} \right)}^2}} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} 13 = {tex}\sqrt {4 + {p^2} - 4p + 144} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} 13 = {tex}\sqrt {{p^2} - 4p + 148} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} 169 = p2 - 4p + 48
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} p2 - 4p - 21 = 0
= p2 - 7p + 3p - 21= 0
= p(p - 7) + 3(p - 7) = 0
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} (p - 7)(p + 3) = 0
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} p = 7, p = -3

questions.question: The distance between the points (x1, y1) and (x2, y2) is given by
questions.explanation: The distance between the points (x1, y1) and (x2, y2) is given by{tex}\sqrt {(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2} {/tex} units. This is known as the distance formula.

questions.question: The distance of the point P(x, y) from the origin O(0, 0) is given by
questions.explanation:
The distance of the point P(x, y) from the origin O(0, 0) is
OP = {tex}\sqrt {{{\left( {x - 0} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {y - 0} \right)}^2}} {/tex}
={tex}\sqrt {{x^2} + {y^2}} {/tex} units

questions.question: The values of y for which the distance between the points (2, -3) and (10, y) is 10 units is
questions.explanation:
Let  one point be A(2, -3) and 2nd point is B(10, y) and
distance between point A and point B = AB = 10 units
{tex}\therefore {/tex} AB2 = (x2 - x1)2 + (y2 - y1)2
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} (10)2 = (10 - 2)2 + (y + 3)2
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} 100 = 64 + y2 + 9 + 6y
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} y2 + 6y - 27 = 0
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} y2 + 9y - 3y - 27 = 0
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} (y + 9)(y - 3) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} y = 3 and y = -9

questions.question: The point on the x-axis which is equidistant from the points (2, -5) and (-2, 9) is
questions.explanation:
Let A(2, - 5) and B(-2, 9)
Since the point is on x-axis C (x, 0)
{tex}\therefore {/tex} AC2 = BC2
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} (2 - x)2 + (-5 - 0)2 = (-2 - x)2 + (9 - 0)2
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} 4 + x2 - 4x + 25 = 4 + x2 + 4x + 81
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} -8x = 56
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} x = -7
Therefore, the point on x-axis is (-7, 0)

questions.question: The point on the y-axis which is equidistant from the points (6, 5) and (-4, 3) is
questions.explanation:
Let one point is A(6, 5) and 2nd point is B(-4, 3)
3rd point on y-axis is C(0, y)
Since C is equidistant from A and B, i.e. AC = BC
{tex}\therefore {/tex} AC2 = BC2
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} (6 - 0)2 + (5 - y)2 = (-4 - 0)2 + (3 - y)2
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} 36 + 25 + y2 - 10y = 16 + 9 + y2 - 6y
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} -4y = -36
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} y = 9
Therefore, the point on y-axis is (0, 9)

questions.question: Find the value of k, if the point (0, 2) is equidistant from the points (3, k) and (k, 5)
questions.explanation:
Let point C (0, 2) is equidistant from the points A(3, k) and B(k, 5)
i.e. AC = BC
{tex}\therefore{/tex} AC2 = BC2
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} (3 - 0)2 + (k - 2)2 = (k - 0)2 + (5 - 2)2
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} 9 + k2 + 4 - 4k = k2 + 9
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} 4k = 4
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} k = 1

questions.question: If the point P(x, y) is equidistant from A(5, 1) and B(-1, 5), then
questions.explanation:
Let the point C{tex}\left( {x,y} \right){/tex} is equidistant from the points A (5, 1) and B{tex}\left( { - 1,5} \right).{/tex}

i.e. AC = BC
{tex}\therefore AC^2 = BC^2{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex}{tex}{\left( {5 - x} \right)^2} + {\left( {1 - y} \right)^2} = {\left( { - 1 - x} \right)^2} + {\left( {5 - y} \right)^2}{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex}{tex}25 + {x^2} - 10x + 1 + {y^2}- 2y = 1 + {x^2} + 2x + 25 + {y^2} - 10y{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex}{tex} - 10x - 2y - 2x + 10y = 26 - 26{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex}{tex} - 12x + 8y = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex}{tex} - 12x = - 8y{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex}{tex}3x = 2y{/tex}

questions.question: If the point P(2, 4) lies on a circle, whose centre is C(5, 8), then the radius of the circle is
questions.explanation:
The point P(2, 4) is on the circle and C(5, 8) is its centre
Hence PC will be Radius of circle.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} PC2 = (2 - 5)2 + (4 - 8)2
PC = {tex}\sqrt {9 + 16} {/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt {25} {/tex} = 5 units

questions.question: The vertices of a square are (0, -1), (2, 1), (0, 3) and (-2, 1). The side of the square is
questions.explanation:
Let the vertices of a square ABCD are A(0, -1), B (2, 1), C (0, 3) and D(-2, 1).
Since all sides of a square are equal i.e.  AB = BC = CD = DA.
{tex}\therefore {/tex} AB = {tex}\sqrt {{{\left( {2 - 0} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {1 + 1} \right)}^2}} {/tex}
={tex}\sqrt {4 + 4} {/tex}
={tex}2\sqrt 2 {/tex} units

questions.question: The perimeter of a triangle with vertices (0, 4), (0, 0) and (3, 0) is
questions.explanation:
Given: the vertices of a triangle ABC, A(0, 4), B (0, 0) and C (3, 0).
{tex}\therefore {/tex} Perimeter of triangle ABC = AB + BC + AC
= {tex}\sqrt{(0-0)^2+(0-4)^2}{/tex}+{tex} \sqrt{0-3)^2+(0-0)^2}{/tex} +{tex} \sqrt{0-3)^2+(4-0)^2}{/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt {0 + 16} + \sqrt {9 + 0} + \sqrt {9 + 16} {/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt {16} + \sqrt 9 + \sqrt {25} {/tex}
= 4 + 3 + 5 = 12 units

questions.question: AOBC is a rectangle whose three vertices are A(0, 3), O(0, 0) and B(5, 0). The length of its diagonal is
questions.explanation:
In rectangle AOBC, AB is a diagonal.
{tex}\therefore {/tex} AB = {tex}\sqrt {{{\left( {5 - 0} \right)}^2} + {{\left( { 0- 3} \right)}^2}} {/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt {25 + 9} {/tex} = {tex}\sqrt {34} {/tex} units

questions.question: (0, 3), (4, 0) and (– 4, 0) are the vertices of
questions.explanation:
Let vertices of a triangle ABC are A (0, 3), B{tex}\left( { - 4,0} \right){/tex} and C (4, 0).
{tex}\therefore {/tex} AB = {tex}\sqrt {{{\left( { - 4 - 0} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {0 - 3} \right)}^2}} {/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt {16 + 9} {/tex} = {tex}\sqrt {25} {/tex} = 5 units
BC = {tex}\sqrt {{{\left( {4 + 4} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {0 - 0} \right)}^2}} {/tex} = {tex}\sqrt {64 + 0} {/tex} = {tex}\sqrt {64} {/tex} = 8 units
AC = {tex}\sqrt {{{\left( {4 - 0} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {0 - 3} \right)}^2}} {/tex} = {tex}\sqrt {16 + 9} {/tex} = {tex}\sqrt {25} {/tex} = 5 units
Since, two sides are equal, therefore, ABC is an isosceles triangle.

questions.question: Radius of circumcircle of a triangle ABC is {tex}5\sqrt {10} {/tex} units. If point P is equidistant from A (1, 3), B(-3, 5) and C(5, -1) then AP =
questions.explanation:
Since P is equidistant from A, B and C.
Therefore, P is centre of circumcircle of triangle ABC.
Hence, AP = Radius of circumcircle = {tex}5\sqrt {10} {/tex} units

questions.question: The distance between the points (a, a) and {tex}( - \sqrt 3 a,\sqrt 3 a){/tex} is
questions.explanation:
Let the points be A{tex}\left( {a,a} \right){/tex} and B{tex}( - \sqrt 3 a,\sqrt 3 a){/tex}
{tex}\therefore {/tex}AB = {tex}\sqrt {{{\left( { - \sqrt 3 a - a} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {\sqrt 3 a - a} \right)}^2}} {/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt {3{a^2} + {a^2} + 2\sqrt 3 a a+ 3{a^2} + {a^2} - 2\sqrt 3 a a} {/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt {6{a^2} + 2{a^2}} {/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt {8{a^2}} {/tex}
= {tex}2a\sqrt 2{/tex} units

questions.question: The distance between the points (sin{tex}\theta{/tex}, cos{tex}\theta{/tex}) and (cos{tex}\theta{/tex}, – sin{tex}\theta{/tex}) is
questions.explanation:
Distance between {tex}\left( {\sin \theta ,\cos \theta } \right){/tex} and {tex}\left( {\cos \theta , - \sin \theta } \right){/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt {{{\left( {\cos \theta - \sin \theta } \right)}^2} + {{\left( { - \sin \theta - \cos \theta } \right)}^2}} {/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt {{{\cos }^2}\theta + {{\sin }^2}\theta - 2\cos \theta \sin \theta + {{\cos }^2}\theta + {{\sin }^2}\theta + 2\cos \theta \sin \theta } {/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt {2{{\cos }^2}\theta + 2{{\sin }^2}\theta } {/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt {2\left( {{{\cos }^2}\theta + {{\sin }^2}\theta } \right)} {/tex}
{tex}\left[ {\because {{\cos }^2}\theta + {{\sin }^2}\theta = 1} \right]{/tex}
= {tex} \sqrt{2}{/tex} units

questions.question: The distance between the points A(p sin25o, 0) and B(0, p sin65o) is
questions.explanation:
The distance between point A{tex}\left( {p\sin 25^\circ ,0} \right){/tex} and point B{tex}\left( {0,p\sin 65^\circ } \right){/tex}=
AB = {tex}\sqrt {{{\left( {0 - p\sin 25^\circ } \right)}^2} + {{\left( {p\sin 65^\circ - 0} \right)}^2}} {/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt {{p^2}{{\sin }^2}25^\circ + {p^2}{{\sin }^2}65^\circ } {/tex}
= {tex}p\sqrt {{{\sin }^2}25^\circ + {{\sin }^2}\left( {90^\circ - 25^\circ } \right)} {/tex}
= {tex}p\sqrt {{{\sin }^2}25^\circ + {{\cos }^2}25^\circ } {/tex}{tex}\left[ {\because \sin \left( {{{90}^ \circ } - \theta } \right) = \cos \theta } \right]{/tex}
= p units
{tex}\left[ {\because {{\cos }^2}\theta + {{\sin }^2}\theta = 1} \right]{/tex}

questions.question: The distance between (at2, 2at) and {tex}\left( {\frac{a}{{{t^2}}},\frac{{ - 2a}}{t}} \right){/tex} is
questions.explanation:
The distance between {tex}(a{t^2},2at){/tex} and {tex}\left( {\frac{a}{{{t^2}}},\frac{{ - 2a}}{t}} \right){/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt {{{\left( {\frac{a}{{{t^2}}} - a{t^2}} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {\frac{{ - 2a}}{t} - 2at} \right)}^2}} {/tex}
= {tex}a\sqrt {\frac{1}{{{t^4}}} + {t^4} - 2 + \frac{4}{{{t^2}}} + 4{t^2} + 8} {/tex}
= {tex}a\sqrt {\frac{1}{{{t^4}}} + {t^4} + \frac{4}{{{t^2}}} + 4{t^2} + 6} {/tex}
= {tex}a\sqrt {\frac{1}{{{t^4}}} + {t^4} + 4 + 2 + \frac{4}{{{t^2}}} + 4{t^2}} {/tex}
= {tex}a\sqrt {{{\left( {{t^2} + \frac{1}{{{t^2}}} + 2} \right)}^2}} {/tex}
= {tex}a\left( {{t^2} + \frac{1}{{{t^2}}} + 2} \right){/tex}
= {tex}a{\left( {t + \frac{1}{t}} \right)^2}{/tex} units

questions.question: If the coordinates of a point are (3, -7), then its ordinate is
questions.explanation:
Since y-coordinate of a point is called ordinate. Its distance from the x-axis measured parallel to the y-axis
Therefore, the ordinate is -7.

questions.question: If the coordinates of a point are (-5, 11), then its abscissa is
questions.explanation:
Since x-coordinate of a point is called abscissa.
Therefore, the abscissa is -5.

questions.question: The abscissa of any point on the y-axis is
questions.explanation:
Since coordinates of any point on y-axis is (0, y)
Therefore, the abscissa is 0.

questions.question: The abscissa of any point on the x-axis is
questions.explanation:
Since coordinates of any point on the x-axis is (x, 0)
Therefore, abscissa is x.

questions.question: The distance of a point from the y-axis is called
questions.explanation: The distance of a point from the y-axis is the x (horizontal)  coordinate of the point and is called abscissa.

questions.question: The distance of a point from the x-axis is called
questions.explanation: The distance of a point from the x-axis is the y (vertical) coordinate of the point and is called ordinate.

questions.question: The point of intersection of the x-axis and y-axis is called
questions.explanation:
The point of intersection of the x-axis and y-axis is called an origin.
The coordinates of the origin are (0, 0).

questions.question: Points (1, 0) and (-1, 0) lies on
questions.explanation: Since the ordinates of given points are 0. Therefore, points lie on x-axis.

questions.question: In the fourth quadrant,
questions.explanation:
In the fourth quadrant, x is positive, y is negative.
i.e the value of x is called abscissa which is positive and the value of y is called coordinate which is negative in the 4th quadrant

questions.question: ________ is an algebraic tool for studying geometry.
questions.explanation: Coordinate Geometry is an algebraic tool for studying geometry.

questions.question: The distance between the points (cos{tex}\theta{/tex}, sin{tex}\theta{/tex}) and (sin{tex}\theta{/tex}, - cos{tex}\theta{/tex}) is
questions.explanation:
Distance between {tex}(\cos \theta, \sin \theta){/tex} and {tex}(\sin \theta,-\cos \theta){/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{\left(x_{2}-x_{1}\right)^{2}+\left(y_{2}-y_{1}\right)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{(-\cos \theta-\sin \theta)^{2}+(\sin \theta-\cos \theta)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{1+1}=\sqrt{2}{/tex} {tex}\left\{\because \sin ^{2} \theta+\cos ^{2} \theta=1\right\}{/tex}

questions.question: The distance between the points (a cos 25°, 0) and (0, a cos 65°) is
questions.explanation:
Distance between (a cos 25o, 0) and (0, a cos65o)
{tex}=\sqrt{\left(x_{2}-x_{1}\right)^{2}+\left(y_{2}-y_{1}\right)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{\left(0-a \cos 25^{\circ}\right)^{2}+\left(a \cos 65^{\circ}-0\right)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{a^{2} \cos ^{2} 25^{\circ}+a^{2} \cos ^{2} 65^{\circ}}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{a^{2}\left[\cos ^{2} 25^{\circ}+\cos ^{2} 65^{\circ}\right]}{/tex}
{tex}=a \sqrt{\cos ^{2}\left(90^{\circ}-65^{\circ}\right)+\cos ^{2} 65^{\circ}}{/tex}
{tex}=a \sqrt{\sin ^{2} 65^{\circ}+\cos ^{2} 65^{\circ}}{/tex}
{tex}=a(\sqrt{1})=a{/tex}

questions.question: If x is a positive integer such that the distance between points P (x, 2) and Q (3, - 6) is 10 units, then x =
questions.explanation:
Distance between P(x, 2) and Q(3, -6) = 10 units
{tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt{\left(x_{2}-{x}_{1}\right)^{2}+\left(y_{2}-y_{1}\right)^{2}}=10{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt{(3-x)^{2}+(-6-2)^{2}}=10{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt{(3-x)^{2}+(-8)^{2}}=10{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt{(3-x)^{2}+64}=10{/tex}
Squaring both sides,
(3 - x)2 + 64 = 100
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 9 + x2 - 6x + 64 - 100 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 - 6x - 27 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 - 9x + 3x - 27 = 0 {tex}\left\{\begin{aligned} \because-27 &=-9 \times 3 \\ -6 &=-9+3 \end{aligned}\right\}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x(x - 9) + 3(x - 9) = 0
(x - 9) (x + 3) = 0
Either x - 9 = 0, then x = 9 or x + 3 = 0, then x = -3
x is positive integer
Hence x = 9

questions.question: The distance between the points (a cos {tex}\theta{/tex} + b sin {tex}\theta{/tex}, 0) and (0, a sin {tex}\theta{/tex} - b cos {tex}\theta{/tex}), is
questions.explanation:
Distance between {tex}(a \cos \theta+b \sin \theta, 0){/tex} and {tex}(0, a \sin \theta-b \cos \theta){/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{\left(x_{2}-x_{1}\right)^{2}+\left(y_{2}-y_{1}\right)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt[]{\sqrt{(0-(a \cos \theta+b \sin \theta))^{2}+\{(a \sin \theta-b \cos \theta-0)\}^{2}}}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{\{0-(a \cos \theta+b \sin \theta)\}^{2}+\{(a \sin \theta-b \cos \theta-0)\}^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{\left(\begin{array}{ll} {a^{2} \cos ^{2} \theta+b^{2} \sin ^{2} \theta+2 a b \sin \theta \cos \theta+} \\ {a^{2} \sin ^{2} \theta+b^{2} \cos ^{2} \theta-2 a b \sin \theta \cos \theta} \end{array}\right)}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{a^{2} \times 1+b^{2} \times 1}=\sqrt{a^{2}+b^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\left\{\because \sin ^{2} \theta+\cos ^{2} \theta=1\right\}{/tex}

questions.question: If the distance between the points A(4, p) and B(1, 0) is 5 units, then the value(s) of p is (are)
questions.explanation:
Distance between (4, p) and (1, 0) = 5
{tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt{\left(x_{2}-x_{1}\right)^{2}+\left(y_{2}-y_{1}\right)^{2}}=5{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt{(1-4)^{2}+(0-p)^{2}}=5{/tex}
{tex}\sqrt{(-3)^{2}+(-p)^{2}}=5{/tex}
Squaring, both sides
(-3)2 + (-p)2 = (5)2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 9 + p2 = 25
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p2 = 25 - 9 = 16
{tex}\therefore p=\pm \sqrt{16}=\pm 4{/tex}

questions.question: A line segment is of length 10 units. If the coordinates of its one end are (2, - 3) and the abscissa of the other end is 10, then its ordinate is
questions.explanation:
Let the ordinate of other end = y
then distance between (2, -3) and (10, y) = 10 units
{tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt{\left(x_{2}-x_{1}\right)^{2}+\left(y_{2}-y_{1}\right)^{2}}=10{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt{(10-2)^{2}+(y+3)^{2}}=10{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt{(8)^{2}+(y+3)^{2}}=10{/tex}
Squaring both sides
(8)2 + (y + 3)2 = (10)2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 64 + (y + 3)2 = 100
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (y + 3)2 = 100 - 64 = 36 = (6)2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (y + 3)2 - (6)2 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (y + 3 + 6) (y + 3 - 6)
= 0 {{tex}\because{/tex} a2 - b2 = (a + b) (a - b)}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (y + 9) (y - 3) = 0
Either y + 9 = 0, then y = -9
or y - 3 = 0, then y = 3
{tex}\therefore{/tex} y = 3, -9

questions.question: The perimeter of the triangle formed by the points (0, 0), (1, 0) and (0, 1) is
questions.explanation:
Let the vertices of {tex}\triangle \mathrm{ABC}{/tex} be A(0, 0), B(1, 0) and C(0, 1)
Now length of AB {tex}=\sqrt{(1-0)^{2}+(0-0)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{(1)^{2}+0^{2}}=\sqrt{1^{2}}=1{/tex}
Length of AC {tex}=\sqrt{(0-0)^{2}+(1-0)^{2}}=\sqrt{0^{2}+(1)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{1^{2}}=1{/tex}
and length of BC {tex}=\sqrt{(0-1)^{2}+(1-0)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{(1)^{2}+(1)^{2}}=\sqrt{1+1}=\sqrt{2}{/tex}
Perimeter of {tex}\triangle \mathrm{ABC}{/tex} = Sum of sides
{tex}=1+1+\sqrt{2}=2+\sqrt{2}{/tex}

questions.question: If three points (0,0), (3, {tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex}) and (3, {tex}\lambda{/tex}) form an equilateral triangle, then {tex}\lambda{/tex} =
questions.explanation:
Let the points (0, 0), {tex}(3, \sqrt{3}){/tex} and {tex}(3, \lambda){/tex} from an equilateral triangle AB = BC = CA
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AB2 = BC2 = CA2
Now, AB2 = (x2 - x1)2 + (y2 - y1)2
{tex}=(3-0)^{2}+(\sqrt{3}-0)^{2}=(3)^{2}+(\sqrt{3})^{2}{/tex}
= 9 + 3 = 12
{tex}\mathrm{BC}^{2}=(3-3)^{2}+(\lambda-\sqrt{3})^{2}{/tex}
{tex}=(0)^{2}+(\lambda-\sqrt{3})^{2}=(\lambda-3)^{2}{/tex}
and {tex}\mathrm{CA}^{2}=(0-3)^{2}+(0-\lambda)^{2}=(-3)^{2}+(-\lambda)^{2}{/tex}
{tex}=9+\lambda^{2}{/tex}
{tex}A B^{2}=C A^{2} \Rightarrow 12=9+\lambda^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \lambda^{2}=12-9=3{/tex}
{tex}\therefore~ \lambda=\pm \sqrt{3}{/tex}

questions.question: The coordinates of the point on x -axis which are equidistant from the points (-3, 4) and (2, 5) are
questions.explanation:
Let the point be A(a, 0) be equidistant from the two given points P(-3, 4) and Q(2, 5) So applying distance formula, we get,
AP2 = AQ2
Therefore,
(a + 3)2 + (-4)2 = (a - 2)5 + 52
10a = 4
{tex}a=\frac{2}{5}{/tex}
Hence the coordinates of A are {tex}\left(\frac{2}{5}, 0\right){/tex}
So the answer is none of these.

questions.question: If A(5, 3), B(11, -5) and P(12, y) are the vertices of a right triangle right angled at P, then y =
questions.explanation:
A(5, 3), B(11, -5) and P(12, y) are the vertices of a right triangle, right-angled at P
{tex}\therefore{/tex}  AB2 = (x2 - x1)2 + (y2 - y1)2  [BY P.G.T]
= (11 - 5)2 + (-5 - 3)2 = (6)2 + (-8)2
= 36 + 64 = 100
Similarly BP2 = (12 - 11)2 + (y + 5)2 = (1)2 + y2 + 10y + 25
= y2 + 10y + 26
and AP2 = (12 - 5)2 + (y - 3)2 = (7)2 + (y - 3)2
= 49 + y2 - 6y + 8 = y2 - 6y + 58
{tex}\because \triangle \mathrm{ABP}{/tex} is a right triangle
{tex}\therefore{/tex} AB2 = BP2 + AP2
100 = y2 + 10y + 26 + y2 - 6y + 58
100 = 2y2 + 4y + 84
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2y2 + 4y + 84 - 100 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2y2 + 4y - 16 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y2 + 2y - 8 = 0 (Dividing by 2)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y2 + 4y - 2y - 8 = 0 {tex}\left\{\begin{array}{l} {\because-8=4 \times(-2)} \\ {2=4-2} \end{array}\right\}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y(y + 4) -2(y + 4) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (y + 4) (y - 2) = 0
Either y + 4 = 0, then y = -4
or y - 2 = 0, then y = 2
y = 2, -4

questions.question: The distance of the point (4, 7) from the x-axis is
questions.explanation: The distance of the point (4, 7) from x-axis = 7

questions.question: The distance of the point (4, 7) from the y-axis is
questions.explanation: The distance of the point (4, 7) from y-a x is = 4

questions.question: If P is a point on x-axis such that its distance from the origin is 3 units, then the coordinates of a point Q on OY such that OP = OQ, are
questions.explanation:
P is a point on x-axis and its distance from 0 is 3
Co-ordinates of P will be (3, 0)
Q is a point on OY such that OP = OQ
Co-ordinates of Q will be (0, 3)

questions.question: If the point (x, 4) lies on a circle whose centre is at the origin and radius is 5 then x =
questions.explanation:
Point A(x, 4) is on a circle with centre O(0, 0) and radius = 5
{tex}\therefore \mathrm{OA}=\sqrt{(x-0)^{2}+(4-0)}=\sqrt{x^{2}+16}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \sqrt{x^{2}+16}=5 \Rightarrow x^{2}+16=25{/tex}
Squaring on both sides, we get
{tex}\Rightarrow x^{2}=25-16=9=(\pm 3)^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore~ x=\pm 3{/tex}

questions.question: If points A(5, p), B(1, 5), C(2, 1) and D(6, 2) form a square ABCD, then p =
questions.explanation:
Vertices of a square are A(5, p), B(1, 5), C(2, 1) and D(6, 2).
The diagonals bisect each other at O
O is the mid-point of AC and BD
O is mid-point of BD, then
CO-ordinates of O will be {tex}\left(\frac{1+6}{2}, \frac{5+2}{2}\right){/tex}
or {tex}\left(\frac{7}{2}, \frac{7}{2}\right){/tex}
{tex}\because {/tex} O is mid-point of AC also
{tex}\therefore \frac{p+1}{2}=\frac{7}{2} \Rightarrow p+1=7{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p = 7 - 1 = 6

questions.question: The point on the x-axis which is equidistant from points (-1, 0) and (5, 0) is
questions.explanation:
Let the required point be P(x, 0). Then,
{tex}P A^{2}=P B^{2} \Rightarrow(x+1)^{2}=(x-5)^{2}\\ \Rightarrow x^{2}+2 x+1=x^{2}-10 x+25\\ \Rightarrow 12 x=24 \Rightarrow x=2{/tex}
So, the required point is P(2, 0).

questions.question: ABCD is a rectangle whose three vertices are B (4,0), C (4,3) and D (0,3). The length of one of its diagonals is
questions.explanation:
Three vertices of a rectangle ABCD are B (4,0), C (4, 3) and D (0, 3) length of one of its diagonals
{tex}\mathrm{BD}=\sqrt{(4-0)^{2}+(0-3)^{2}}=\sqrt{4^{2}+3^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{16+9}=\sqrt{25}{/tex} = 5

questions.question: The distance of the point P(-6, 8) from origin is:
questions.explanation:
The distance of the point P( -6,8) from the origin (0, 0)
{tex}=\sqrt{(-6)^{2}+8^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{36+64}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{100}{/tex}
= 10

questions.question: The distance of the point P(-3, 4) from x-axis is
questions.explanation:

= Y- coordinate of the point 
= 4 units

questions.question: Which point on x-axis is equidistant from the points A(7, 6) and B(-3, 4)?
questions.explanation:
Let the required point be P(x, 0) then, 
{tex}A P^{2}=B P^{2} \Rightarrow(x-7)^{2}+(0-6)^{2}-(x+3)^{2}+(0-4)^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x^{2}-14 x+85=x^{2}+6 x+25{/tex}
{tex}-20 x-60=x=3{/tex}

questions.question: The points (-4, 0), (4, 0) and (0, 3) are the vertices of a:
questions.explanation:
AB2 = (4 + 4)2 + (0 - 0)2 = 82 + 02 = 64 + 0 = 64
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AB={tex}\sqrt{64}{/tex}= 8 units
BC2 = (0-4)2 + (3-0)2 = (-4)2 + 32 = 16 + 9 = 25
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} BC = {tex}\sqrt{25}{/tex} = 5 units.
AC2 = (0 + 4)2 + (3 - 0)2 = 42 + 32 = 16 + 9 = 25
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A C = {tex}\sqrt{25}{/tex} = 5 units.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC is isosceles.

questions.question: The points P(0, 6), Q(-5, 3) and R( 3,1) are the vertices of a triangle, which is
questions.explanation:
{tex}PQ=\sqrt{(-5)^{2}+(-3)^{2}}=\sqrt{2 5+3}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{34}{/tex}

{tex}\triangle{/tex}PQR is a Isosceles triangle.

questions.question: If the distance between the points (2, -2) and (-1, x) is 5, one of the values of x is
The points A(9, 0), B(9, 6), C(-9, 6) and D(-9, 0) are the vertices of a
questions.explanation:
A (9, 0), B(9, 6), C( - 9, 6) and D( - 9, 0) are the given vertices.
Then,
AB2 = (9 - 9)2 + (6 - 0)2 
= (0)2 + (6)2  = 0 + 36  = 36 units
BC2 = ( - 9 - 9)2 + (6 - 6)2 
= (-18)2 + (0)2  = 324 + 0 = 324 units
CD2 = (- 9 + 9)2 + (0 - 6)2 = (0)2 + (-6)2 = 0 + 3  = 36 units
DA2 = ( - 9 - 9)2 + (0 - 0)2 = (- 18)2 + (0)2 = 324 + 0  = 324 units
Therefore, we have:
AB2 = CD2 and BC2 = DA2
Now, the diagonals are:
AC2 = ( - 9 - 9)2 + (6 - 0)2 = (- 18)2 + (6)2 = 324 + 36 = 360 units
BD2 = ( - 9 - 9)2 + (0 - 6)2 = ( - 18)2 + ( - 6)2 = 324 + 36 = 360 units
Therefore,
AC2 = BD2 
Hence, ABCD is a rectangle.

questions.question: The distance between the points A (0, 6) and B (0, –2) is
questions.explanation:
By using the distance formula:

d2 = (x2 - x1)2 + (y2 - y1)2
Lets calculate the distance between the points (x1, y1) and (x2, y2)
We have;
x1 = 0, x2 = 0
y1 = 6, y2 = - 2
d2 = (0 – 0)2 + ( - 2 - 6)2 
d = {tex}\sqrt{(0)^{2}+(-8)^{2}}{/tex} 
d = {tex}\sqrt{64}{/tex}
d = 8 units
So, the distance between A (0, 6) and B (0, - 2) = 8

questions.question: The distance between the points (0, 5) and (–5, 0) is
questions.explanation:
By using the formula:
d2 = (x2 - x1)2 + (y2 - y1)2 

To calculate distance between the points (x1 , y1) and (x2, y2)
Here we have;
x1 = 0, x2 = - 5
y2 = 5, y2 = 0
d2 = [( - 5) - 0]2 + [0 - 5]2 
d = {tex}\sqrt{(-5-0)^{2}+(0-5)^{2}}{/tex} 
d = {tex}\sqrt{25+25}{/tex} 
d = {tex}\sqrt{50}{/tex} = 5{tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex}

So, the points whose abscissa and ordinate have different signs are 2nd and 4th quadrants.

questions.question: The ordinate of the point on the y-axis, which is equidistant from (-4, 1) and (2, 3) is:

A(2, 3) and B(-4, 1) are the given points.
Let C(0.y) be the points are y-axis
AC = {tex}\sqrt{(0-2)^{2}+(y-3)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AC = {tex}\sqrt{4+y^{2}+9-6 y}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AC = {tex}\sqrt{y^{2}-6 y+13}{/tex}
BC = {tex}\sqrt{(0+4)^{2}+(y-1)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} BC = {tex}\sqrt{16+y^{2}+1-2 y}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} BC = {tex}\sqrt{y^{2}-2 y+17}{/tex}
Since AC = BC
AC2 = BC2
y2 - 6y + 13 = y2 - 2y + 17
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -6y + 2y = 17 - 13
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -4y = 4
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = -1
Therefore, the point on y-axis is (0, -1) and here ordinate is -1.

questions.question: If {tex}\left(1, \frac{\sqrt{7}}{3}\right){/tex} is one of the ends of a diameter, then its other end is

{tex}\therefore\left(\frac{1+x}{2}, \frac{\frac{\sqrt{7}}{3}+y}{2}\right){/tex} = (0, 0)
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1+x}{2}{/tex} = 0 and {tex}\frac{\frac{\sqrt{7}}{3}+y}{2}{/tex} = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = -1 and y = {tex}-\frac{\sqrt{7}}{3}{/tex}
Thus, the coordinates of other end be {tex}\left(-1, \frac{-\sqrt{7}}{3}\right){/tex}.

questions.question: Points (6, 8), (3, 7), (-2, -2) and (1, -1) are joined to form a quadrilateral. What will be the structure of the quadrilateral?

Let the points be A(6, 8), B(3, 7), C(-2, -2) and D(1, -1).

Now, AB = {tex}\sqrt{(3-6)^2+(7-8)^2}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt {10}{/tex}
BC = {tex}\sqrt{(-2-3)^2+(-2-7)^2}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt {106}{/tex}
CD = {tex}\sqrt{(1+2)^2+(-1+2)^2}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt {10}{/tex}
DA = {tex}\sqrt{(6-1)^2+(8+1)^2}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt {106}{/tex}
Also, AC = {tex}\sqrt{(-8)^2+(-10)^2}{/tex}  = {tex}\sqrt{64+100}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{164}{/tex}
BD = {tex}\sqrt{(-2)^2+(-8)^2}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{4+64}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{68}{/tex}
Since, AB = DC and BC = DA and AC {tex}\neq{/tex} BD.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} ABCD is a parallelogram.

questions.question: The coordinates of the third vertex of an equilateral triangle whose two vertices are at (3, 4), (-2, 3), are ________.
questions.explanation:
Since, {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC is an equilateral triangle.

{tex}\therefore{/tex} AB = AC {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AB2 = AC2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - 3)2 + (y - 4)2
= (x + 2)2 + (y - 3)2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5x + y - 6 = 0 ...(i)
Now, area of equilateral triangle
= {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} (side)2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} (BC)2 = {tex}\frac{13 \sqrt{3}}{2}{/tex}units
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{13 \sqrt{3}}{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac 12{/tex}|x(1) + 3(3 - y) - 2(y - 4)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\pm{/tex}13{tex}\sqrt 3{/tex} = x + 9 - 3y - 2y + 8
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x - 5y + 17 = {tex}\pm{/tex}13{tex}\sqrt 3{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x - 5y = 13{tex}\sqrt 3{/tex} - 17 ...(ii)
or x - 5y = -(13{tex}\sqrt 3{/tex} + 7) ...(iii)
Solving (i) and (ii), we get,
x = {tex}\frac{1+\sqrt{3}}{2}{/tex}, y = {tex}\frac{7-5 \sqrt{3}}{2}{/tex}
Also, on solving (i) and (iii), we get
x = {tex}\frac{1-\sqrt{3}}{2}{/tex}, y = {tex}\frac{5 \sqrt{3}+7}{2}{/tex}
