Radius of circle, OC = {tex}\frac{AB}{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{50}{2}{/tex} = 25 cm
Now, OP = {tex}\sqrt{O C^{2}-C P^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{(25)^{2}-(24)^{2}}{/tex} [using Pythagoras theorem in right angled {tex}\triangle{/tex}OPC]
= {tex}\sqrt{625-576}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{49}{/tex} = 7

questions.question: In a circle of radius 5 cm, AB and AC are two chords such that AB = AC = 6 cm. Find the length of the chord BC.
questions.explanation:
Let O be the centre of the circle.
Let the bisector of {tex}\angle{/tex}BAC meet the chord BC at M.

In {tex}\triangle{/tex}BAM and {tex}\triangle{/tex}CAM,
{tex}\angle{/tex}BAM = {tex}\angle{/tex}MAC [by construction]
AB = AC [given]
AM = AM [common side]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}BAM {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}CAM [by SAS congruence rule]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} BM = MC and {tex}\angle{/tex}BMA = {tex}\angle{/tex}AMC [by CPCT]
But {tex}\angle{/tex}BMA + {tex}\angle{/tex}AMC = 180o [{tex}\because{/tex} BMC is a straight line]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}BMA = 90o
Therefore, AM is perpendicular bisector of BC and hence it passes through the centre O of the circle.
Let BM = y cm and OM = x cm, then
MA = OA  - OM = (5 - x) cm [{tex}\because{/tex} radius = 5 cm]
In right angled {tex}\triangle{/tex}OMB, y2 + x2 = 52 ...(i)
In right angled {tex}\triangle{/tex}BMA, y2 + (5 - x)2 = 62
i.e. y2 + x2 - 10x = 11 ...(ii) [by Pythagoras theorem]
On subtracting Eq. (ii) from Eq. (i), we get
10x = 25 - 11 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 10x = 14 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\frac75{/tex}
On substituting the value of x in Eq. (i), we get
y2 + {tex}\frac{19}{25}{/tex} = 25 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y2 = 25 - {tex}\frac{49}{25}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{625-49}{25}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y2 = {tex}\frac{576}{25}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = {tex}\frac{24}{5}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Length of chord BC = 2BM = {tex}2 \cdot \frac{24}{5}{/tex} cm = {tex}\frac{48}{5}{/tex} cm = 9.6 cm

questions.question: If two circles intersect in two points, then the line through their centres to the bisector of common chord is
questions.explanation:
Given that the two circles with centres C, D and intersecting at points A, B so that AB is their common chord.

In {tex}\triangle{/tex}ACD and {tex}\triangle{/tex}BCD
CA = CB [radii of same circle]
AD = BD [radii of same circle]
CD = CD [common side]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}ACD {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}BCD [by SSS congruence rule]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}ACM = {tex}\angle{/tex}MCB [by CPCT]
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}ACM and {tex}\triangle{/tex}BCM,
{tex}\angle{/tex}ACM = {tex}\angle{/tex}MCB
CA = CB [radii of same circle]
CM = CM [common side]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}ACM {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}BCM [by SAS congruence rule]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AM = MB and {tex}\angle{/tex}CMA = {tex}\angle{/tex}CMB [by CPCT]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}CMA + {tex}\angle{/tex}CMB = 180o [{tex}\because{/tex} AMB is a straight line]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}CMA = 90o [{tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}CMA = {tex}\angle{/tex}CMB]
Hence, CD is perpendicular bisector of AB.

questions.question: Two congruent circles intersect each other at points A and B. Through A, any line segment PAQ is drawn, so that P and Q lie on the two circles. Then,
questions.explanation:
Let O' and O be the centres of two congruent circles. Since, AB is a common chord of these circles.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}BPA = {tex}\angle{/tex}BQA [since, angles subtended by equal chords are equal]

Hence, BP = BQ [{tex}\because{/tex} sides opposite to equal angles of a triangle are also equal]

questions.question: Two chords AB and CD of lengths 5 cm and 11 cm, respectively of a circle are parallel to each other and are on opposite sides of its centre. If the distance between AB and CD is 6 cm, then find the radius of the circle.
questions.explanation:
Let O be the centre of the given circle and its radius be b cm. Given, AB = 5 cm and CD = 11 cm are two chords on opposite sides of its centre. Draw ON {tex}\perp{/tex} AB and OM {tex}\perp{/tex} CD. Since, ON {tex}\perp{/tex} AB, OM {tex}\perp{/tex} CD and AB {tex}\parallel{/tex} CD, therefore the points N, O and M are collinear.

Given, distance between AB and.CD = MN = 6 cm
Let ON = a cm
{tex}\therefore{/tex} OM = (6 - a) cm
Join OA and OC.
Then, OA = OC = b cm
We know that the perpendicular from the centre to a chord of the circle bisects the chord.
Then, AN = NB = {tex}\frac52{/tex} = 2.5 cm
and CM = MD = {tex}\frac{11}{2}{/tex} = 5.5 cm
In right angled {tex}\triangle{/tex}ONA and {tex}\triangle{/tex}OMC,
OA2 = ON2 + AN2
and OC2 = OM2 + CM2 [by Pythagoras theorem]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b2 = a2 + (2.5)2 ...(i)
and b2 = (6 - a)2 + (5.5)2 ...(ii)
So, a2 + (2.5)2 = (6 - a)2 + (5.5)2 [from Eqs. (i) and (ii)]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a2 + 6.25 = 36 - 12a + a2 + 30.25
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 12a = 60 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 5
On putting a = 5 in Eq. (i), we get
b2 = (5)2 + (2.5)2
= 25 + 6.25 = 31.25
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Radius = b = {tex}\sqrt{31.25}{/tex} = 5.6 cm (approx.).

questions.question: The diameter of the circle
questions.explanation:
The diameter of the circle
= MS {{tex}\angle{/tex}MRS = 90o, angle in the semi-circle}
= {tex}\sqrt{\mathrm{MR}^{2} +\mathrm{RS}^{2}}=\sqrt{6^{2}+8^{2}}=\sqrt{36+64}=\sqrt{100}{/tex} = 10 m

questions.question: Measure of {tex}\angle{/tex}MRS is
questions.explanation: {tex}\angle{/tex}MRS = 90o (Angle in the semi-circle)

questions.question: Area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}MRS is
questions.explanation: Are of {tex}\triangle{/tex}MRS = {tex}\frac 12{/tex} (MR) (RS) =  {tex}\frac 12{/tex} (6) (8) = 24 m2

questions.question: Length of the longest chord
questions.explanation: Length of the longest chord = length of diameter = 10 m

questions.question: Radius of the circle is
questions.explanation: Radius of the circle = {tex}\frac 12{/tex} (diameter) = {tex}\frac 12{/tex} (10) = 5 m

questions.question: In the given figure, if chords and CD of the circle intersect each other at right angles, then x + y = ________.
questions.explanation:
{tex}\angle{/tex}CDB = {tex}\angle{/tex}CAB (Angles in same segment are equal)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}CDB = x
Given, {tex}\angle{/tex}CPB = 90°
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}DPB, {tex}\angle{/tex}PDB + {tex}\angle{/tex}PBD = {tex}\angle{/tex}CPB (exterior angle property)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x + y = 90°

questions.question: In the given figure, angles subtended by chords AC and BC at the centre O of the circle are 55° and 155° respectively. Find {tex}\angle{/tex}ACB.
questions.explanation:
Given, {tex}\angle{/tex}AOC = 55°, {tex}\angle{/tex}BOC = 155°
reflex {tex}\angle{/tex}AOB = {tex}\angle{/tex}AOC + {tex}\angle{/tex}BOC = 55° + 155° = 210°
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}AOB = 360° -210 ° = 150°
Since angle subtended by an arc at centre is double the angle subtended by it on the remaining part of the circle.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}ACB = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}AOB = 75°

questions.question: In the given figure, if PQRS is a cyclic quadrilateral with respective angles. Then, the ratio of x and y is ________.
questions.explanation:
Given, PQRS is a cyclic quadrilateral.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}PSR + {tex}\angle{/tex}PQR = {tex}\angle{/tex}QPS + {tex}\angle{/tex}QRS = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2y + 3y = 2x + 4x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5y = 6x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{x}{y}=\frac{5}{6}{/tex} i.e., 5 : 6

questions.question: O is the centre of the circle. If {tex}\angle{/tex}POQ = 98°, then {tex}\angle{/tex}PRQ is ________.
questions.explanation:
Since angle subtended by an arc at centre is double the angle subtended by it on the remaining part of the circle.
{tex}\therefore \quad \angle P R Q=\frac{1}{2} \angle P O Q=\frac{1}{2} \times 98^{\circ}=49^{\circ}{/tex}

questions.question: In the given figure, AB = 8 cm, OM = ON = 4 cm. Then CD is ________.
questions.explanation:
Given, AB = 8 cm and OM = ON = 4 cm
Since equal chords are equidistant from the centre.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} CD = 8 cm

questions.question: In the given figure, O is the centre of the circle and {tex}\angle{/tex}BAC = 56°. The measure of {tex}\angle{/tex}BDC is ________.
questions.explanation:
Given, {tex}\angle{/tex}BAC = 56°
Since, angles in same segment are equal.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}BAC = {tex}\angle{/tex}BDC = 56°

questions.question: In the given figure, {tex}\angle{/tex}ABD = 70°, {tex}\angle{/tex}ADB = 30°. Then, {tex}\angle{/tex}BCD is ________.
questions.explanation:
Given, {tex}\angle{/tex}ABD = 70°, {tex}\angle{/tex}ADB = 30°
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}ADB, {tex}\angle{/tex}DAB + {tex}\angle{/tex}ADB + {tex}\angle{/tex}ABD = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}DAB + 30° + 70° = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}DAB = 180° - 100° = 80°
Since ABCD is a cyclic quadrilateral.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}DAB + {tex}\angle{/tex}BCD = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 80° + {tex}\angle{/tex}BCD =180° {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}BCD = 100°

questions.question: In the given figure, if {tex}\angle{/tex}DAB = 62° and {tex}\angle{/tex}ABD=58°, then {tex}\angle{/tex}ACB is equal to______ .
questions.explanation:
Given, {tex}\angle{/tex}DAB = 62°, {tex}\angle{/tex}ABD = 58°
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}ADB, {tex}\angle{/tex}DAB + {tex}\angle{/tex}ABD + {tex}\angle{/tex}ADB = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 62° + 58° + {tex}\angle{/tex}ADB = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}ADB = 180° - 120° = 60°
Now, {tex}\angle{/tex}ACB = {tex}\angle{/tex}ADB = 60° (Angles in same segment are equal)

questions.question: AD is a diameter of a circle and AB is a chord. If AD = 34 cm, AB = 30 cm, then the distance of AB from the centre of the circle is _______.
questions.explanation:
Given, AD = 34 cm, AB = 30 cm
{tex}\therefore \quad A O=\frac{1}{2} A D=\frac{1}{2}(34)=17 \mathrm{~cm}{/tex}
Draw OP {tex}\perp{/tex} AB.
Since, perpendicular drawn from centre to the chord bisects the chord.
{tex}\therefore \quad A P=\frac{1}{2} A B=15 \mathrm{~cm}{/tex}

Now, in right angled {tex}\triangle{/tex}APO
(OP)2 = (17)2 - (15)2 = 289 - 225 = 64
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} OP = 8 cm
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Distance of AB from the centre of the circle is 8 cm.

questions.question: In the given figure, {tex}\angle{/tex}PQR = 150°, where P, Q and R are points on a circle with centre O. Then {tex}\angle{/tex}OPR is ________.
questions.explanation:
Given, {tex}\angle{/tex}PQR = 150°
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Reflex {tex}\angle{/tex}POR = 2{tex}\angle{/tex}PQR = 2(150°) = 300°
Now, {tex}\angle{/tex}POR = 360° - Reflex {tex}\angle{/tex}POR = 360° - 300° = 60° ...(i)
Also, OP = OR {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}OPR = {tex}\angle{/tex}ORP ...(ii) (Angles opposite to equal sides of a triangle are equal)
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}OPR, {tex}\angle{/tex}OPR + {tex}\angle{/tex}ORP + {tex}\angle{/tex}POR = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2{tex}\angle{/tex}OPR + 60° = 180° [From (i) & (ii)]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2{tex}\angle{/tex}OPR = 120° {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}OPR = 60°

questions.question: PQRS is a cyclic quadrilateral such that PR is a diameter of the circle. If {tex}\angle{/tex}QPR = 67° and {tex}\angle{/tex}SPR = 72°, then {tex}\angle{/tex}QRS =______.
questions.explanation:
{tex}\angle{/tex}QPS = {tex}\angle{/tex}QPR + {tex}\angle{/tex}SPR = 67° + 72° = 139°
PQRS is a cyclic quadrilateral.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}QRS + {tex}\angle{/tex}QPS = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}QRS= 180°-139° = 41°

questions.question: In the given figure, PQRS is a cyclic quadrilateral in which PS = RS, {tex}\angle{/tex}SQR = x and {tex}\angle{/tex}PQS = 60°. The value of x is_______.
questions.explanation:
: Given, PS = RS, {tex}\angle{/tex}PQS = 60° and ZSQR = x
Since, angles in the same segment are equal.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}SPR = {tex}\angle{/tex}SQR = x
Also, ZSPR = ZSRP ({tex}\because{/tex} PS = RS)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}SRP = x
{tex}\angle{/tex}SRP = {tex}\angle{/tex}PQS (Angles in the same segment are equal)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 60°

questions.question: A, B, C and D are four points on a circle. AC and BD intersect at a point E such that {tex}\angle{/tex}BEC = 130° and {tex}\angle{/tex}ECD = 20°, then {tex}\angle{/tex}BAC is ________.
questions.explanation:
Given, {tex}\angle{/tex}BEC = 130° and {tex}\angle{/tex}ECD = 20°
Now, {tex}\angle{/tex}ABD = {tex}\angle{/tex}ACD (Angles in the same segment)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}ABD = 20° Now, in {tex}\triangle{/tex}AEB
{tex}\angle{/tex}EBA + {tex}\angle{/tex}BAE = {tex}\angle{/tex}BEC (exterior angle property)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 20° + ZBAC = 130° {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} ZBAC = 110°

questions.question: In the given figure, O is the centre of the circle. Find the values of x, y and z.
questions.explanation:
Since ABCD is a cyclic quadrilateral
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x + 3x = 180° {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4x = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 45°
Now, {tex}\angle{/tex}BOD = 2{tex}\angle{/tex}BAD ({tex}\because{/tex} Angle subtended by an arc at the centre is double the angle subtended by it at any point on the remaining part of the circle)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} y = 2x = 2 x 45° = 90°
Similarly, z = 2(3x) = 6 x 45° = 270°.

questions.question: In the given figure, AEDF is a cyclic quadrilateral. The values of x and y respectively are
questions.explanation:
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}AEB, {tex}\angle{/tex}ABE + {tex}\angle{/tex}BEA + {tex}\angle{/tex}BAE = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 35° + {tex}\angle{/tex}BEA + 54° = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}BEA = 91°
Now, {tex}\angle{/tex}AFD + {tex}\angle{/tex}DEA = 180° (opposite angles of cyclic quadrilateral)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x + 91° = 180° => x = 89°
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}AFC, {tex}\angle{/tex}AFC + {tex}\angle{/tex}FCA + {tex}\angle{/tex}CAF = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 89° + y + 54° = 180° {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 37°

questions.question: O is the centre of the circle having radius 5 cm. AB and AC are two chords such that AB = 6 cm. If OA meets BC at P, then OP = ________.
questions.explanation:
We have, AB = AC = 6 cm
OB = OC = 5 cm
{tex}\therefore{/tex} ABOC is a kite.
Since, diagonals of kite are perpendicular to one another.
{tex}\angle{/tex}OPB = {tex}\angle{/tex}OPC = 90°
Let OP = x {tex}\therefore{/tex} AP = O A - OP = 5 - x ...(i)
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}OPB, (OB)2 = (OP)2 + (PB)2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (5)2 = x2 + (PB)2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (PB)2 = 25 -x2 ...(ii)
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}APB, (AB)2 = (AP)2 + (PB)2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (6)2 = (5 - x)2 + (PB)2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (PB)2 = (6)2 - (5 - x)2 ...(iii)
From (ii) and (iii), we have
25 - x2 = (6)2 - (5 - x)2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 25 - x2 = 36 - 25 - x2 + 10x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 14 = 10x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 1.4 cm

questions.question: In the given figure, if O is the centre of the circle, then x = ________.
questions.explanation:
Since, angle subtended by an arc at centre is double the angle subtended by it on the remaining part of the circle.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}AOB = 2{tex}\angle{/tex}ACB = 2 x 29° = 58° {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 58°

questions.question: In the given figure, AOB is the diameter of a circle and CD || AB. If {tex}\angle{/tex}BAD = 30°, then {tex}\angle{/tex}CAD =_____.
questions.explanation:
Given that, CD || AB and {tex}\angle{/tex}BAD = 30°
{tex}\because{/tex} AOB is the diameter.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}ADB = 90° (Angle in semicircle)
Now, In {tex}\triangle{/tex}ADB, {tex}\angle{/tex}DAB + {tex}\angle{/tex}ADB + {tex}\angle{/tex}ABD = 180°
30° + 90° + {tex}\angle{/tex}ABD = 180° {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}ABD = 60°
In cyclic quadrilateral ABDC, we have
{tex}\angle{/tex}ABD + {tex}\angle{/tex}ACD = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 60° + {tex}\angle{/tex}ACD = 180° {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}ACD = 120°
Also, {tex}\angle{/tex}CDA = 30° = {tex}\angle{/tex}DAB (Alternate angles)
In A CAD, {tex}\angle{/tex}CAD + {tex}\angle{/tex}CDA + {tex}\angle{/tex}ACD = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}CAD + 30° + 120° = 180° {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}CAD = 30°

questions.question: In the given figure, a circle touches all the four sides of a quadrilateral ABCD whose three sides are AB = 6 cm, BC = 7 cm, CD = 4 cm, then AD equals ________.
questions.explanation:
x + y = 6 cm ...(i)
y + z = 7 cm ...(ii)
z + w = 4 cm ...(iii)
Using (ii) and (iii),
y - w = 3 cm
Now using (i);
x + (3 + w) = 6 cm
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x + w = 3 cm
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AD = 3 cm

questions.question: In the given figure, AB and PQ intersect at M. If A and B are centres of circles, then ________.
questions.explanation:
In the given figure, PQ is the common chord of the two circles.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AB bisects the common chord PQ at M.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} PM = MQ
Moreover, PQ is perpendicular to AB.

questions.question: In the given figure, O is the centre of the circle, then {tex}\angle{/tex}XOZ is ________.
questions.explanation:
OX = OY = OZ = r (radii of circle)
Taking {tex}\angle{/tex}OXY = {tex}\theta{/tex} and {tex}\angle{/tex}OZY = {tex}\alpha{/tex}
Then in {tex}\triangle{/tex}YXO, OX = OY {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}OXY = {tex}\angle{/tex}OYX = {tex}\theta{/tex}
Now, in {tex}\triangle{/tex}OZY, OZ = OY {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}OZY = {tex}\angle{/tex}OYZ = {tex}\alpha{/tex}
Now, XOZY is a quadrilateral.
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle O X Y+\angle X Y Z+\angle O Z Y+\angle X O Z=360^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \theta+\theta+\alpha+\alpha+\angle X O Z=360^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle X O Z=360^{\circ}-2(\alpha+\theta){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle X O Z=360^{\circ}-2(\angle O Z X{/tex}{tex}+\angle X Z Y+\angle O X Z+\angle Z X Y){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle X O Z=360^{\circ}-2(\angle O Z X+\angle O X Z){/tex}{tex}-2(\angle X Z Y+\angle Z X Y){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle X O Z=360^{\circ}-2{/tex}{tex}\left(180^{\circ}-\angle X O Z\right)-2(\angle X Z Y+\angle Z X Y){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle X O Z=360^{\circ}-360^{\circ}{/tex}{tex}+2 \angle X O Z-2(\angle X Z Y+\angle Z X Y){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle X O Z=2(\angle X Z Y+\angle Z X Y){/tex}

questions.question: In the given figure, A and B are the centres of two circles that intersect at X and Y. PXQ is a straight line. If reflex angle QBY = 210o, then find obtuse angle PAY.
questions.explanation:
In quadrilateral YBQX

{tex}\angle{/tex}YXQ = {tex}\frac 12{/tex} reflex {tex}\angle{/tex}QBY = {tex}\frac 12{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 210o = 105o
PXQ is straight line 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}PXY + {tex}\angle{/tex}YXQ = 180o (linear pair)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}PXY = 180o - 105o = 75o
Now, in quadrilateral PAYX
{tex}\angle{/tex}PZY = {tex}\frac 12{/tex}{tex}\angle{/tex}PAY
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}PAY = 2{tex}\angle{/tex}PZY = 150o

questions.question: OACB is a quadrant of a circle with centre O and radius 7 cm where ACB is the arc. Then the perimeter of the quadrant is:
The length of an arc of a circle with radius 12 cm is 10{tex}\pi{/tex} cm. The central angle subtended by this arc at the centre, is:
questions.explanation:
Radius of circle = 12 cm
Arc length = 10 {tex}\pi{/tex} cm
And
Arc length = {tex}\frac{\theta}{360} \times 2 \pi r{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 10 \pi =\frac{\theta}{360} \times 2 \times \pi \times 12{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \theta=150^{\circ}{/tex}

questions.question: What geometric locus represents all possible centers of circles that pass through two given points, X and Y?

options.option: A circle with XY as its diameter
The perpendicular bisector of the line segment XY

questions.explanation: Any point equidistant from two given points X and Y lies on the perpendicular bisector of the line segment XY. Since the center of a circle passing through X and Y must be equidistant from X and Y, all such centers lie on the perpendicular bisector of XY.

questions.question: Consider a circle with center O. If two chords, MN and XY, are of equal length, how do the angles they subtend at the center compare?

options.option: The comparison depends on the specific location of the chords.
Angle MON is greater than angle XOY.
Angle MON is equal to angle XOY.
Angle XOY is greater than angle MON.

questions.explanation:
Equal chords of a circle subtend equal angles at the center.
Since chords MN and XY are of equal length, the angles they subtend at the center (angle MON and angle XOY) must be equal.

questions.question: In a circle, if a chord forms an isosceles triangle with the center of the circle, what kind of lines are the sides of the triangle connecting the chord's endpoints to the center?
questions.explanation: The lines connecting the chord's endpoints to the center are always the radii of the circle. Since the radii from the center to any point on the circle's circumference are equal, the triangle formed by the chord and two radii will always be isosceles.

questions.question: If two chords of a circle subtend central angles of different measures, what can be inferred about their lengths?

options.option: The chord subtending the smaller angle is longer.
The chord subtending the larger angle is longer.
The chords must be of equal length.

questions.explanation: If the central angles are different, the chords must also have different lengths. Specifically, the chord subtending a larger angle at the center will be longer.

questions.question: What is the term for the circle that passes through the vertices of a triangle?
questions.explanation: The circle that passes through all three vertices of a triangle is known as the circumcircle. Its center is called the circumcentre, and the triangle is said to be inscribed in the circle.

questions.question: In a circle with center P, triangles PRQ and PST are formed by chords RQ and ST, respectively. If PR = PQ = PS = PT = radius of the circle and angle RPQ = angle SPT, what can be concluded about the chords RQ and ST?

options.option: Chord ST is longer than chord RQ.
Chord RQ is longer than chord ST.
Chords RQ and ST are equal in length.
The relationship between their lengths cannot be determined.

questions.explanation: Given that PR = PQ, PS = PT (all radii), and angle RPQ = angle SPT, by the SAS (Side-Angle-Side) congruence criterion, triangle PRQ is congruent to triangle PST. Therefore, their corresponding sides, including the chords RQ and ST, must be equal in length.

questions.question: If three distinct points, P, Q, and R, are collinear, how many unique circles can pass through all three points?
questions.explanation: A fundamental property of circles is that no circle can pass through three distinct collinear points. If three points are on a straight line, they cannot lie on the circumference of a single circle.

questions.question: A carpenter is making a circular tabletop with a radius of 40 cm. He needs to place a reinforcing rod that acts as a line of reflection symmetry. What will be the length of this rod?
questions.explanation:
A line of reflection symmetry for a circle is a diameter.
The diameter is twice the radius.
Radius (r) = 40 cm
Diameter (d) = 2 × r
Diameter (d) = 2 × 40 cm
Diameter (d) = 80 cm
Therefore, the length of the reinforcing rod will be 80 cm.

questions.question: When are two isosceles triangles formed by chords and the center of a circle congruent to each other based on their base lengths?

options.option: They are congruent if their base lengths are equal.
They are congruent if and only if their radii are different.
They are always congruent, regardless of base length.
They are congruent only if their central angles are unequal.

questions.explanation: If two isosceles triangles (formed by chords and the center) have equal base lengths (i.e., equal chord lengths), and the other two sides are radii (which are always equal in a given circle), then by the SSS (Side-Side-Side) congruence criterion, the triangles are congruent.

questions.question: In a circle with a radius of 8 cm, two chords AB and CD are such that they subtend angles of 45 degrees and 60 degrees, respectively, at the center. If a third chord EF subtends an angle of 45 degrees at the center, what is the length relationship of EF compared to AB and CD?

options.option: EF has the same length as AB.
EF is shorter than AB and also shorter than CD.
EF has the same length as CD.
EF is longer than AB but shorter than CD.

questions.explanation: The chords that subtend equal angles at the center are equal. Since chord AB subtends an angle of 45 degrees and chord EF also subtends an angle of 45 degrees, their lengths must be equal. Chord CD subtends a 60-degree angle, so its length will be different from AB and EF.

questions.question: A circle is perfectly symmetrical. If you rotate a circle by any angle, it appears exactly the same. What type of symmetry does this characteristic describe?
questions.explanation: The characteristic of a rotating circle looking the same at all times, regardless of the angle of rotation, is described as complete rotational symmetry.

questions.question: A circular garden has two decorative pathways, one formed by chord P1P2 and another by chord P3P4. The length of P1P2 is 12 meters. If the angle subtended by P1P2 at the center of the garden is 80 degrees, and P3P4 also subtends an angle of 80 degrees at the center, what is the length of P3P4?
questions.explanation: The chords of a circle that subtend equal angles at the center are equal. Since both P1P2 and P3P4 subtend an 80-degree angle at the center, their lengths must be equal. Therefore, the length of P3P4 is 12 meters.

questions.question: What is the defining characteristic of points that can serve as the center of a circle passing through two given points A and B?

options.option: They must lie on the line segment connecting A and B.
They must be equidistant from A and B.
They must be closer to A than to B.
They must form a right angle with A and B.

questions.explanation: For a circle to pass through two points A and B, its center must be equidistant from both points. This means the center O will have OA = OB, ensuring both points lie on the circle's circumference at the same radius.

questions.question: Which of the following statements accurately describes the relationship between the circumcentre and the type of triangle it circumscribes?

options.option: For an acute-angled triangle, the circumcentre lies outside the triangle.
For a right-angled triangle, the circumcentre is at the midpoint of the hypotenuse.
For an obtuse-angled triangle, the circumcentre lies inside the triangle.
For any triangle, the circumcentre always lies inside the triangle.

questions.explanation: The circumcentre's position depends on the type of triangle. For an acute-angled triangle, the circumcentre lies inside. For an obtuse-angled triangle, it lies outside. For a right-angled triangle, the circumcentre is precisely at the midpoint of its hypotenuse.

questions.question: A circular clock face has a radius of 10 cm. If a decorative strip is to be placed along a chord that is half the length of the longest possible chord, what is the length of this decorative strip?
questions.explanation:
First, determine the length of the longest possible chord. The longest chord in a circle is its diameter.
The diameter is twice the radius.
Radius (r) = 10 cm
Longest Chord (Diameter) = 2 × r = 2 × 10 cm = 20 cm
The decorative strip is half the length of the longest possible chord.
Length of Decorative Strip = (1/2) × Longest Chord
Length of Decorative Strip = (1/2) × 20 cm
Length of Decorative Strip = 10 cm
Therefore, the length of the decorative strip is 10 cm.

questions.question: A pizza with a diameter of 30 cm is cut into several slices. If a cut is made along the longest possible straight line across the pizza, how long is this cut?
questions.explanation:
The longest possible straight line segment across a circle is its diameter.
Given Diameter = 30 cm
Therefore, the length of the cut is 30 cm.

questions.question: A circular garden has a radius of 12 meters. A designer wants to install a decorative pathway that represents the longest possible straight line segment within the garden. What would be the length of this pathway?
questions.explanation:
The longest possible straight line segment within a circle is its diameter.
The diameter is twice the radius.
Given
Radius (r) = 12 meters
Diameter (d) = 2 × r
Diameter (d) = 2 × 12 meters
Diameter (d) = 24 meters
Therefore, the length of the pathway would be 24 meters.

questions.question: If a line segment within a circle is a line of reflection symmetry, what can we definitively conclude about this segment?

options.option: It is a radius of the circle.
It is a chord that does not pass through the center.
It is a diameter of the circle.
It is a tangent to the circle.

questions.explanation: A line of reflection symmetry for a circle must pass through its center and connect two points on the circumference, making it a diameter.

questions.question: A circle possesses complete rotational symmetry. What does this imply about its appearance after rotation?

options.option: It looks exactly the same after rotation by any angle.
It looks the same only after a 180-degree rotation.
Its appearance changes proportionally to the angle of rotation.
It looks the same only after a 360-degree rotation.

questions.explanation: Complete rotational symmetry means that if you rotate a circle by any angle, it will appear exactly the same as its original orientation.

questions.question: Which of the following statements accurately describes the relationship between the center of a circle and its lines of reflection symmetry?

options.option: Only specific lines of reflection symmetry pass through the center.
Lines of reflection symmetry always start from the circumference but do not necessarily pass through the center.
The center of the circle is irrelevant to its lines of reflection symmetry.
Each line of reflection symmetry for a circle must pass through its center.

questions.explanation: Every line of reflection symmetry in a circle is a diameter, and by definition, all diameters pass through the center of the circle.

questions.question: A wheel has spokes that each measure 35 cm from the center to the rim. What is the total length of the wheel's rim if a single complete spoke defines the radius?
questions.explanation:
A spoke from the center to the rim of a wheel is the radius.
The total length of the wheel's rim refers to its circumference.
The formula for the circumference of a circle is C = 2πr.
Given:
Radius (r) = 35 cm
Circumference (C) = 2πr
C = 2 * (3.14) * 35 cm
C = 219.8 cm
To convert centimeters to meters,
C = 219.8 cm / 100 = 2.198 meters

questions.question: In a wheel of radius 20 cm, a metal rod serves as a chord with a length of 24 cm. If a second, identical wheel has a metal rod that subtends the same angle at its center as the first rod, what is the length of the second rod?
questions.explanation: The chords of a circle that subtend equal angles at the center are equal. Since the second rod subtends the same angle at its center as the first rod (which is 24 cm long), the second rod must also be 24 cm long.

questions.question: Consider a circle with its center at the origin (0, 0). If a point on the circle is (8, 0), what are the coordinates of another point that would form a diameter if connected to (8, 0)?
questions.explanation:
A diameter is a line segment that passes through the center of the circle and has its endpoints on the circle.
If the center is at (0, 0) and one endpoint of the diameter is (8, 0), then the other endpoint must be equidistant from the center in the opposite direction.
Given: Center = (0, 0)
One endpoint of diameter = (8, 0)
Since the center is the midpoint of the diameter, if one coordinate is (x, y), the opposite coordinate will be (-x, -y) when the center is the origin. In this case, for (8,0), the opposite point is (-8, 0).

questions.question: A circular garden has a diameter of 12 meters. What is the radius of this garden?
questions.explanation:
A diameter is a chord that passes through the center of a circle.
The radius is the distance from the center to any point on the circle, which is exactly half of the diameter.
Given: Diameter = 12 meters
Formula: Radius = {tex}\frac{Diameter}{2}{/tex}
Radius {tex}= \frac{12}{2}{/tex} = 6 meters

questions.question: A circular pond has two ropes stretched across it as chords. Rope 1 is 10 feet long and Rope 2 is also 10 feet long. If the radius of the pond is 7 feet, what type of triangles are formed by each rope and the center of the pond, and what relationship exists between these two triangles?

options.option: Right-angled triangles; they are similar but not congruent.
Scalene triangles; they are similar but not congruent.

questions.explanation: Each rope, along with two radii connecting its endpoints to the center, forms a triangle. Since both sides connecting to the center are radii (7 feet each), these triangles are isosceles. Furthermore, since both ropes are 10 feet long (equal bases), by SSS congruence (Radius-Radius-Chord), the two isosceles triangles are congruent.

questions.question: A circle has its center at point M. A chord XY is drawn with its midpoint at N. If the line segment MN is drawn, what is its relationship to the chord XY?

options.option: MN is twice the length of XN.
MN bisects XY at an acute angle.

questions.explanation: The line joining the centre of a circle and the midpoint of a chord of the circle is perpendicular to the chord. Therefore, MN is perpendicular to XY.

subtopics.name: Chords And The Angles They Subtend

questions.question: Consider a circle with center O and a chord PQ. If R is the midpoint of PQ, and the line segment OR is drawn. Which of the following statements is true?

options.option: Triangle ORP is always an equilateral triangle.
OR is parallel to the tangent at P.
Length of OR is equal to the radius.

questions.explanation: The line joining the center of a circle to the midpoint of a chord is perpendicular to the chord. Therefore, OR is perpendicular to PQ, meaning angle ORQ is 90 degrees.

questions.question: If a line segment is drawn from the center of a circle to a chord AB such that it bisects AB, then this line segment is ________ to AB.
questions.explanation: Theorem 4 states that the line joining the centre of a circle and the midpoint of a chord is perpendicular to the chord. Since the line segment bisects AB, it connects to the midpoint of AB, thus making it perpendicular to AB.

questions.question: A circle has a radius of {tex}13 \text{ cm}{/tex}. A chord AB is {tex}24 \text{ cm}{/tex} long. Calculate the distance from the center of the circle to the midpoint of the chord.
questions.explanation:
Let the center of the circle be O and the midpoint of the chord AB be M.
The line segment OM is perpendicular to the chord AB.
We are given: Radius (OA) = {tex}13 \text{ cm}{/tex} Chord length (AB) = {tex}24 \text{ cm}{/tex}
Since M is the midpoint of AB, AM = MB = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times 24 \text{ cm} = 12 \text{ cm}{/tex}.
In the right-angled triangle OMA (angle OMA = 90 degrees):
Using the Pythagorean theorem: {tex}OA^2 = OM^2 + AM^2{/tex}
{tex}(13)^2 = OM^2 + (12)^2{/tex}
{tex}169 = OM^2 + 144{/tex}
{tex}OM^2 = 169 - 144{/tex}
{tex}OM^2 = 25{/tex}
{tex}OM = \sqrt{25}{/tex}
{tex}OM = 5 \text{ cm}{/tex}
Therefore, the distance from the center to the midpoint of the chord is {tex}5 \text{ cm}{/tex}.

questions.question: In a circle with center P, a chord CD is {tex}16 \text{ cm}{/tex} long. If the perpendicular distance from P to CD is {tex}6 \text{ cm}{/tex}, what is the radius of the circle?
questions.explanation:
Let the center of the circle be P and the point where the perpendicular from P meets the chord CD be Q.
The perpendicular from the center of a circle to a chord bisects the chord.
We are given: Chord length (CD) = {tex}16 \text{ cm}{/tex}
Perpendicular distance (PQ) = {tex}6 \text{ cm}{/tex}
Since Q is the midpoint of CD, CQ = QD = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times 16 \text{ cm} = 8 \text{ cm}{/tex}.
In the right-angled triangle PQC (angle PQC = 90 degrees):
Using the Pythagorean theorem (where PC is the radius, r): {tex}PC^2 = PQ^2 + CQ^2{/tex}
{tex}r^2 = (6)^2 + (8)^2{/tex}
{tex}r^2 = 36 + 64{/tex}
{tex}r^2 = 100{/tex}
{tex}r = \sqrt{100}{/tex}
{tex}r = 10 \text{ cm}{/tex}

questions.question: A circle has a diameter of {tex}26 \text{ mm}{/tex}. A chord in this circle is located {tex}5 \text{ mm}{/tex} away from the center. What is the length of this chord?
questions.explanation:
Let the center of the circle be O and the chord be AB.
Let M be the point on AB such that OM is perpendicular to AB.
OM bisects AB.
We are given: Diameter = {tex}26 \text{ mm}{/tex}, so radius (OA) = {tex}\frac{26}{2} = 13 \text{ mm}{/tex}
Distance from center to chord (OM) = {tex}5 \text{ mm}{/tex}
In the right-angled triangle OMA (angle OMA = 90 degrees):
Using the Pythagorean theorem: {tex}OA^2 = OM^2 + AM^2{/tex}
{tex}(13)^2 = (5)^2 + AM^2{/tex}
{tex}169 = 25 + AM^2{/tex}
{tex}AM^2 = 169 - 25{/tex}
{tex}AM^2 = 144{/tex}
{tex}AM = \sqrt{144}{/tex}
{tex}AM = 12 \text{ mm}{/tex}
Since M is the midpoint of AB, the length of the chord AB = {tex}2 \times AM = 2 \times 12 \text{ mm} = 24 \text{ mm}{/tex}.

questions.question: A circle has a radius of {tex}15 \text{ units}{/tex}. A chord is {tex}18 \text{ units}{/tex} long. What is the perpendicular distance from the center of the circle to this chord?
questions.explanation:
Let the center of the circle be O and the chord be AB.
Let M be the midpoint of the chord AB.
The line segment OM is the perpendicular distance from the center to the chord.
We are given: Radius (OA) = {tex}15 \text{ units}{/tex} Chord length (AB) = {tex}18 \text{ units}{/tex}
Since M is the midpoint of AB, AM = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times 18 = 9 \text{ units}{/tex}.
In the right-angled triangle OMA (angle OMA = 90 degrees):
Using the Pythagorean theorem: {tex}OA^2 = OM^2 + AM^2{/tex}
{tex}(15)^2 = OM^2 + (9)^2{/tex}
{tex}225 = OM^2 + 81{/tex}
{tex}OM^2 = 225 - 81{/tex}
{tex}OM^2 = 144{/tex}
{tex}OM = \sqrt{144}{/tex}
{tex}OM = 12 \text{ units}{/tex}

questions.question: A circle has its center at point M(2, 3). If one point on the circle is P(5, 7), what is the length of the radius of this circle?
questions.explanation:
To find the radius, we calculate the distance between the center M(2, 3) and the point P(5, 7) on the circle using the distance formula.
{tex}d = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2}{/tex}
Given: {tex}M(x_1, y_1) = (2, 3){/tex}
{tex}P(x_2, y_2) = (5, 7){/tex}
Substitute the values into the formula: {tex}\text{radius} = \sqrt{(5 - 2)^2 + (7 - 3)^2}{/tex}
{tex}\text{radius} = \sqrt{(3)^2 + (4)^2}{/tex}
{tex}\text{radius} = \sqrt{9 + 16}{/tex}
{tex}\text{radius} = \sqrt{25}{/tex}
{tex}\text{radius} = 5 \text{ units}{/tex}

questions.question: Consider a quadrilateral with vertices at coordinates A(1,1), B(5,1), C(5,5), D(1,5). Can these points be concyclic?

options.option: Yes, but only if its diagonals are equal.
Yes, because the quadrilateral is a square, and the sum of opposite angles is 180°.
No, because they form a square and squares are never cyclic.
No, because only circles can have concyclic points.

questions.explanation: The given coordinates form a square. In a square, all angles are 90°. Therefore, for any pair of opposite angles, their sum will be 90° + 90° = 180°. Since the sum of opposite angles is 180°, the quadrilateral (square) is cyclic according to Theorem 12. In fact, all rectangles, including squares, are cyclic.

questions.question: A quadrilateral PQRS has angles ∠P = 90°, ∠Q = 80°, ∠R = 90°, and ∠S = 100°. Is this quadrilateral cyclic?

options.option: Yes, because all angles are within 0° and 180°.
No, because it has two right angles.
Yes, because both pairs of opposite angles sum to 180°.
No, because only one pair of opposite angles sums to 180°.

questions.explanation:
For a quadrilateral to be cyclic, the sum of opposite angles must be 180° (Theorem 12):

∠P + ∠R = 90° + 90° = 180°

∠Q + ∠S = 80° + 100° = 180°

Since both pairs of opposite angles sum to 180°, the quadrilateral PQRS is cyclic.

questions.question: Given a cyclic quadrilateral JKLM. If ∠J = (2x + 10)° and ∠L = (3x - 20)°. Determine the value of x.
questions.explanation:
In a cyclic quadrilateral, the sum of opposite angles is 180°

So, ∠J + ∠L = 180°

(2x + 10) + (3x - 20) = 180

5x - 10 = 180

5x = 190

x = 190 / 5

x = 38

subtopics.name: Midpoints and Perpendicular Bisectors of Chords

questions.question: A line segment XY subtends an angle of 65° at point P and an angle of 65° at point Q. Both P and Q are on the same side of the line XY. What can be concluded about the points X, Y, P, Q?

options.option: The points X, Y, P, Q are collinear.
The points X, Y, P, Q form a parallelogram.
The points X, Y, P, Q are vertices of a rhombus.
The points X, Y, P, Q are concyclic.

questions.explanation: If a line segment AB joining two points A, B subtends equal angles at two other points C, D that lie on the same side of AB, then the four points lie on a circle. Here, segment XY subtends equal angles (65°) at P and Q, which are on the same side of XY. Therefore, X, Y, P, Q are concyclic.

questions.question: In a quadrilateral ABCD, it is given that ∠A = 70°, ∠B = 100°, ∠C = 110°, and ∠D = 80°. Can this quadrilateral be classified as a cyclic quadrilateral based on its angles?

options.option: No, because the sum of opposite angles is not 180° for all pairs.
Yes, because the sum of all angles is 360°.
Yes, because all angles are less than 180°.
No, because none of the angles are equal.

questions.explanation:
For a quadrilateral to be cyclic, the sum of its opposite angles must be 180°. Let's check:

∠A + ∠C = 70° + 110° = 180°

∠B + ∠D = 100° + 80° = 180°

Since both pairs of opposite angles sum to 180°, this quadrilateral is cyclic.

questions.question: Consider three distinct, non-collinear points P, Q, and R. What can be definitively stated about a circle passing through these points?

options.option: An infinite number of circles can pass through these points.
Exactly one unique circle can pass through these points.
No circle can pass through three non-collinear points.
A circle can pass through them only if they form an equilateral triangle.

questions.explanation: It is a fundamental geometric principle that a unique circle can always be drawn through any three non-collinear points.

questions.question: What is the sum of any pair of opposite angles in a cyclic quadrilateral?
questions.explanation: The sum of two opposite angles of a cyclic quadrilateral is 180°.

questions.question: What is a 'cyclic quadrilateral'?

options.option: A quadrilateral whose vertices all lie on a single circle.
A quadrilateral with exactly two parallel sides.
A quadrilateral whose vertices are all right angles.

questions.explanation: A cyclic quadrilateral is defined as a quadrilateral where all its vertices are concyclic, meaning they all lie on the circumference of the same circle.

questions.question: Which of the following statements correctly defines 'concyclic points'?

options.option: Points that are all located on the circumference of a single circle.
Points that are equidistant from a central point.
Points that lie on the same straight line.

questions.explanation: Concyclic points are defined as points that lie on the same circle. If points are on the same circle, they are concyclic.

questions.question: Two points, M and N, are located at (2, 3) and (8, 3) respectively. What is the equation of the locus of all possible centres for circles passing through M and N?
questions.explanation:
The locus of all possible centers for circles passing through two points M and N is the perpendicular bisector of the line segment MN.
Points are M=(2, 3) and N=(8, 3). 1.
Midpoint {tex}= \left(\frac{x_1+x_2}{2}, \frac{y_1+y_2}{2}\right) = \left(\frac{2+8}{2}, \frac{3+3}{2}\right) = \left(\frac{10}{2}, \frac{6}{2}\right) = (5, 3){/tex}
Find the slope of MN: Slope {tex}= \frac{y_2-y_1}{x_2-x_1} = \frac{3-3}{8-2} = \frac{0}{6} = 0{/tex}
Since the slope is 0, the line segment MN is horizontal.
The perpendicular bisector of a horizontal line is a vertical line passing through the midpoint.
A vertical line has an undefined slope and its equation is of the form {tex}x = c{/tex}.
Since the midpoint is (5, 3), the equation of the perpendicular bisector is {tex}x = 5{/tex}.

questions.question: A triangle has vertices at coordinates (1,1), (5,1), and (3,7). If its circumcentre is located at (3, k), what is the value of k?
questions.explanation:
Let the vertices be A = (1, 1), B = (5, 1), and C = (3, 7).
Let the circumcentre be O = (3, k).
The circumcentre is equidistant from all vertices, so OA = OB = OC.
Calculate the square of the distance from O to A and O to B: {tex}OA^2 = (3-1)^2 + (k-1)^2 {/tex} {tex}= 2^2 + (k-1)^2 = 4 + (k-1)^2{/tex}
{tex}OB^2 = (3-5)^2 + (k-1)^2 {/tex} {tex}= (-2)^2 + (k-1)^2 = 4 + (k-1)^2{/tex}
As expected, {tex}OA^2 = OB^2{/tex}.
Now, calculate the square of the distance from O to C: {tex}OC^2 = (3-3)^2 + (k-7)^2 {/tex} {tex}= 0^2 + (k-7)^2 = (k-7)^2{/tex}
Set {tex}OA^2 = OC^2{/tex}
{tex}4 + (k-1)^2 = (k-7)^2{/tex}
{tex}4 + k^2 - 2k + 1 = k^2 - 14k + 49{/tex}
{tex}k^2 - 2k + 5 = k^2 - 14k + 49{/tex}
Subtract {tex}k^2{/tex} from both sides: {tex}-2k + 5 = -14k + 49{/tex}
Add {tex}14k{/tex} to both sides: {tex}12k + 5 = 49{/tex}
Subtract 5 from both sides: {tex}12k = 44{/tex}
{tex}k = \frac{44}{12} = \frac{11}{3}{/tex}
Thus, k is not 1, 2, or 3.

questions.question: A right-angled triangle has sides of length 6 cm, 8 cm, and 10 cm. What is the radius of its circumcircle?
questions.explanation:
For a right-angled triangle, the circumcentre lies at the midpoint of its hypotenuse.
The hypotenuse is the longest side, which in this case is 10 cm.
The radius of the circumcircle is half the length of the hypotenuse.
Given the sides are 6 cm, 8 cm, and 10 cm, the hypotenuse is 10 cm.
Radius of circumcircle {tex}= \frac{\text{Hypotenuse}}{2} = \frac{10}{2} = 5{/tex} cm.

questions.question: A circle passes through two points, A and B, such that the segment AB acts as its diameter. If A is at (0, 0) and B is at (6, 8), what is the center of this circle?
questions.explanation:
If the segment AB is the diameter of a circle, then the center of the circle must be the midpoint of AB.
Given points A = (0, 0) and B = (6, 8).
Coordinates of the midpoint {tex}= \left(\frac{x_1+x_2}{2}, \frac{y_1+y_2}{2}\right){/tex}
Center {tex}= \left(\frac{0+6}{2}, \frac{0+8}{2}\right) = \left(\frac{6}{2}, \frac{8}{2}\right) = (3, 4){/tex}

questions.question: Three non-collinear points are given as (1, 1), (5, 1), and (3, 3). How many unique circles can be drawn that pass through all three of these points?
questions.explanation:
There is a unique circle that passes through any three distinct non-collinear points.
Given that the points (1, 1), (5, 1), and (3, 3) are non-collinear, exactly one unique circle can be drawn through them.

questions.question: Points P, Q, and R are non-collinear. If the perpendicular bisector of PQ is represented by the equation y = x + 1, and the perpendicular bisector of QR is represented by y = -x + 5, what are the coordinates of the circumcenter of triangle PQR?
questions.explanation:
The circumcenter of a triangle is the point where the perpendicular bisectors of its sides intersect.
To find the circumcenter, we need to solve the system of equations for the two perpendicular bisectors.
Given equations: 1) {tex}y = x + 1{/tex} 2) {tex}y = -x + 5{/tex}
Set the two expressions for y equal to each other: {tex}x + 1 = -x + 5{/tex}
Add {tex}x{/tex} to both sides: {tex}2x + 1 = 5{/tex}
Subtract 1 from both sides: {tex}2x = 4{/tex}
Divide by 2: {tex}x = 2{/tex}
Substitute the value of {tex}x{/tex} into either equation to find {tex}y{/tex}.
Using equation (1): {tex}y = x + 1{/tex}
{tex}y = 2 + 1{/tex}
{tex}y = 3{/tex}
Therefore, the coordinates of the circumcenter are (2, 3).

questions.question: A circle has a diameter AB, where A = (2, 5) and B = (10, 5). What is the radius of the circle?
questions.explanation:
The length of the diameter AB can be found using the distance formula between points A(2, 5) and B(10, 5).
Distance {tex}AB = \sqrt{(x_2-x_1)^2 + (y_2-y_1)^2}{/tex}
{tex}AB = \sqrt{(10-2)^2 + (5-5)^2}{/tex}
{tex}AB = \sqrt{(8)^2 + (0)^2}{/tex}
{tex}AB = \sqrt{64}{/tex}
{tex}AB = 8{/tex} units
Since AB is the diameter, the radius is half of the diameter.
Radius {tex}= \frac{AB}{2} = \frac{8}{2} = 4{/tex} units.

questions.question: The circumcentre of an acute-angled triangle XYZ is located at (4, -2). If the triangle is scaled uniformly from the origin by a factor of 2, what will be the coordinates of the new circumcentre?
questions.explanation:
When a geometric figure, including its special points like the circumcentre, is scaled uniformly from the origin by a certain factor, the coordinates of every point are multiplied by that scaling factor.
Original circumcentre coordinates = (4, -2)
Scaling factor = 2 New x-coordinate {tex}= 4 \times 2 = 8{/tex}
New y-coordinate {tex}= -2 \times 2 = -4{/tex}
Therefore, the coordinates of the new circumcentre will be (8, -4).

questions.question: Two points, P and Q, are 14 cm apart. If a circle is to pass through both P and Q, what is the smallest possible radius that this circle can have?

options.option: There is no smallest radius; it can be arbitrarily close to 0.

questions.explanation:
For a circle to pass through two points P and Q, the shortest distance between these points, which is the length of the segment PQ, will be the diameter of the smallest possible circle.
Thus, the radius of the smallest circle is half the length of PQ.
Given the distance between P and Q = 14 cm.
Diameter of the smallest circle = 14 cm.
Radius = {tex}\frac{\text{Diameter}}{2} = \frac{14}{2} = 7{/tex} cm.

questions.question: A circle has two chords, PQ and RS. If the central angle subtended by chord PQ is 70 degrees and the central angle subtended by chord RS is also 70 degrees, what can be concluded about the lengths of the chords?

options.option: The lengths of the chords cannot be determined without knowing the radius.
The chords PQ and RS are equal in length.
Chord RS is longer than chord PQ.
Chord PQ is longer than chord RS.

questions.explanation: If chords of a circle subtend equal angles at the centre, then the chords are equal. Since both chords subtend an angle of 70 degrees, their lengths must be equal.

subtopics.name: Area of a Triangle - by Heron's Formula (IX-10.1)

questions.question: The measure of each side of an equilateral triangle whose area is {tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex} cm2 is
questions.explanation:
Area of equilateral triangle = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}a^2}{4}{/tex} where a = side of the triangle
{tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}a^2}{4}{/tex}
Solving
a2 = 4
a = 2 cm

questions.question: The perimeter of a triangle is 60cm. If its sides are in the ratio 1:3:2, then its smallest side is
questions.explanation:
Given: Ratio of sides: 1 : 3 : 2
Let the sides of triangle be x, 3x and 2x cm
Perimeter = 60 cm
x + 3x + 2x = 60
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 6x = 60
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 10 
So, sides are
a = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 = 10 cm
b = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 = 30 cm
c = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 = 20 cm
Therefore, Length of smallest side = 10 cm.

questions.question: The side of a triangle is 12 cm, 16 cm, and 20 cm. Its area is
questions.explanation:
{tex}s = {{12 + 16 + 20} \over 2} = {{48} \over 2}{/tex} = 24 cm
Area of triangle  = {tex}\sqrt {s\left( {s - a} \right)\left( {s - b} \right)\left( {s - c} \right)} {/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt {24\left( {24 - 16} \right)\left( {24 - 12} \right)\left( {24 - 20} \right)} {/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt {24 \times 8 \times 12 \times 4} {/tex} = 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 8 = 96 sq cm

questions.question: If side of equilateral triangle is 25 m. Its area is
questions.explanation:
Area of equilateral triangle = {tex}{{\sqrt 3 } \over 4}{\left( {{\rm{Side}}} \right)^2}{/tex} 
= {tex}{{\sqrt 3 } \over 4}{\left( {{\rm{25}}} \right)^2}{/tex}
= {tex}{{625\sqrt 3 } \over 4}{/tex}  sq.cm

questions.question: The perimeter of an equilateral triangle is 48 cm. Its area is
questions.explanation:
Side = {tex}{{48} \over 3}{/tex} = 16 cm
Area ={tex}{{\sqrt 3 } \over 4} \times 16 \times 16{/tex} = {tex}64\sqrt 3 {/tex} sq cm

questions.question: The area of a triangle with base 8 cm and height 10 cm is
questions.explanation:
Area = {tex}{1 \over 2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} Base {tex}\times{/tex} Height
= {tex}{1 \over 2} \times 8 \times 10{/tex} 
= 40 cm2

questions.question: The area of a triangle is 150 cm2 and its sides are in the ratio 3 : 4 : 5. What is its perimeter?
A triangle ABC in which AB = AC = 4 cm and {tex}\angle A{/tex} = 90o, has an area of
questions.explanation:
According to question, in given right-angled triangle AB and AC are Base and Perpendicular respectively.
Therefore,
Area = {tex}{1 \over 2} \times 4 \times 4{/tex} 
= 8 cm2

questions.question: If the side of an equilateral triangle is 4 cm, then its area is
questions.explanation:
Area of equilateral triangle = {tex}{{\sqrt 3 } \over 4}{/tex}a2
= {tex}{{\sqrt 3 } \over 4} \times 4 \times 4{/tex}
= {tex}4\sqrt 3 {/tex} cm2

questions.question: If the perimeter of an equilateral triangle is 24 m, then its area is
questions.explanation:
Side = {tex}{{24} \over 3}{/tex} = 8m
Area = {tex}{{\sqrt 3 } \over 4} \times 8 \times 8{/tex} 
= {tex}16\sqrt 3 {/tex} m2

questions.question: If the area of an isosceles right triangle is 8 cm2, what is the perimeter of the triangle?
questions.explanation:
Let each of the two equal sides of an isosceles right triangle be a cm
Then, third side = {tex}a \sqrt{2}{/tex}m
Area of {tex}\Delta=\frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times 2{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 8=\frac{a^{2}}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a2 = 16
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 4 cm
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Perimeter
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a + a + a {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} = 4 + 4 + 4 {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} = 8 + 4{tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} cm2

questions.question: The area of a right-angled triangle if the radius of its circumcircle is 3 cm and altitude drawn to the hypotenuse is 2 cm. is
questions.explanation:
Since in a right-angled triangle, the circumcentre is the mid-point of the hypotenuse, then
Hypotenuse = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 = 6 cm
Now, Area of right-angled triangle = {tex}{1 \over 2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} Base {tex}\times{/tex} Altitude
= {tex}{1 \over 2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 6 sq. cm

questions.question: Area of an isosceles triangle ABC with AB = a = AC and BC = b is
questions.explanation:
Here s = {tex}\frac{a + a + b}{2} = \frac{2a + b} {2}{/tex} 
Area of triangle = {tex}\sqrt {s\left( {s - a} \right)\left( {s - b} \right)\left( {s - c} \right)} {/tex} 
= {tex}\sqrt{\frac{2 a+b}{2}\left(\frac{2 a+b}{2}-a\right)\left(\frac{2 a+b}{2}-a\right)\left(\frac{2 a+b}{2}-b\right)}{/tex} 
= {tex}\sqrt{\frac{2 a+b}{2}\left(\frac{b}{2}\right)\left(\frac{b}{2}\right)\left(\frac{2 a-b}{2}\right)}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{b}{4}\sqrt {4{a^2} - {b^2}} {/tex}

questions.question: An isosceles right triangle has area 8 cm2. The length of its hypotenuse is
questions.explanation:
Area of isosceles triangle = {tex}{1 \over 2}{/tex} {tex}\times {/tex} Base {tex}\times {/tex} Height
Since in an isosceles triangle, Base and Height are equal.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 8 = {tex}{1 \over 2}{/tex} {tex}\times {/tex} Base {tex}\times {/tex} Base
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Base = Height = 4 cm
Hypotenuse = {tex}\sqrt {{4^2} + {4^2}} {/tex} = {tex}\sqrt {32} {/tex} cm

questions.question: The perimeter of an equilateral triangle is 60 m. The area is
questions.explanation:
Perimeter of equilateral triangle = 60 m
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3 {tex}\times{/tex} side = 60 m
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} side = 20 m
Area of equilateral triangle = {tex}{{\sqrt 3 } \over 4}{\left( {{\rm{Side}}} \right)^2}{/tex}
= {tex}{{\sqrt 3 } \over 4}20 \times 20{/tex}
= {tex}100\sqrt 3 {/tex} sq.m

questions.question: The area of an equilateral triangle with side {tex}2\sqrt 3 {/tex} cm is
questions.explanation:
Area of equilateral triangle = {tex}{{\sqrt 3 } \over 4}{\left( {{\rm{Side}}} \right)^2}{/tex}

= {tex}{{\sqrt 3 } \over 4} \times 2\sqrt 3 \times 2\sqrt 3 {/tex}

= {tex}3\sqrt 3 {/tex}

= 3 x 1.732 = 5.196 sq. cm

questions.question: The length of each side of an equilateral triangle having an area of {tex}9\sqrt 3 {/tex} {tex}c{m^2}{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
Area of equilateral triangle = {tex}9\sqrt 3 {/tex} sq cm
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}{{\sqrt 3 } \over 4}{\left( {{\rm{Side}}} \right)^2} = 9\sqrt 3 {/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (Slide)2 = 36
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Side = 6 cm

questions.question: If the area of an equilateral triangle is {tex}16\sqrt 3 \;c{m^2}{/tex}, then the perimeter of the triangle is
questions.explanation:
Area of equilateral triangle = {tex}{{\sqrt 3 } \over 4}{\left( {{\rm{Side}}} \right)^2} = 16\sqrt 3 {/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}{\left( {{\rm{Side}}} \right)^2} = 64{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Side = 8 cm
Perimeter of equilateral triangle = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} side
= 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 8 = 24 cm

questions.question: The sides of a triangle are 35 cm, 54 cm and 61 cm respectively, and its area is {tex}420\sqrt 5 {/tex} cm2. The length of its longest altitude is
questions.explanation:
Since longest altitude is drawn opposite to the shortest side in a triangle.
Area of triangle = {tex}{1 \over 2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} Base {tex}\times{/tex} Height
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}420\sqrt 5 = {1 \over 2} \times 35{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} Height
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Height = {tex}{{420\sqrt 5 \times 2} \over {35}} = 24\sqrt 5 {/tex} cm

questions.question: The difference of semi-perimeter and the sides of {tex}\triangle ABC{/tex} are 8, 7 and 5 cm respectively. Its semi-perimeter ‘s’ is
questions.explanation:
Given: s - a = 8 cm, s - b = 7 cm and s - c = 5 cm
Adding all equations,
s - a + s - b + s - c = 8 + 7 + 5
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3s - (a + b + c) = 20 [s ={tex}\frac{a+b+c}{2}{/tex}]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3s - 2s = 20
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} s = 20 cm

questions.question: The product of difference of semi-perimeter & respective sides of {tex} \triangle ABC{/tex} are given as 13200 {tex}{m^2}{/tex}.The area of {tex}\triangle ABC{/tex} , if its semi-perimeter is 132 m, is given by
questions.explanation:
Given: {tex}\left( {s - a} \right)\left( {s - b} \right)\left( {s - c} \right) = 13200{/tex} m and s = 132 m

Area of triangle = {tex}\sqrt {s\left( {s - a} \right)\left( {s - b} \right)\left( {s - c} \right)} {/tex}

= {tex}\sqrt {13200 \times 132} {/tex}

= 1320 sq. m

questions.question: The area of an isosceles right angled triangle of equal side 30 cm, is given as
questions.explanation:
Area of triangle = {tex}{1 \over 2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} Base {tex}\times{/tex} Height
= {tex}{1 \over 2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 30 {tex}\times{/tex} 30 = 450 sq. cm

questions.question: The area of one triangular part of a rhombus ABCD is given as 125 cm2. The area of rhombus ABCD is
questions.explanation:
Since diagonals of a rhombus divide it into 4 triangles of equal area. Therefore,
Area of rhombus = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} Area of triangle
= 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 125 = 500 sq. cm

questions.question: Each side of an equilateral triangle measures 10 cm. Then the area of the triangle is
questions.explanation:
Area of equilateral trianlge = {tex}{{\sqrt 3 } \over 4}{\left( {{\rm{Side}}} \right)^2}{/tex} 
= {tex}{{1.732} \over 4} \times 10 \times 10{/tex} 
= 43.3 sq. cm

questions.question: The length of each side of an equilateral triangle of area {tex}4 \sqrt{3}{/tex} cm2, is
questions.explanation:
If side of an equilateral triangle is 'a', then its
Area = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} a^{2}{/tex} 
Now {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} a^{2}{/tex} = 4{tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex}
a2 = 4​​​​2
a = 4cm

questions.question: The area of an isosceles triangle having base 2 cm and the length of one of the equal sides 4 cm, is
questions.explanation:
s = {tex}\frac{4 + 4 + 2}{2}{/tex}= 5 cm
Area of triangle = {tex}\sqrt {s\left( {s - a} \right)\left( {s - b} \right)\left( {s - c} \right)} {/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt {5\left( {5 - 4} \right)\left( {5 - 4} \right)\left( {5 - 2} \right)} {/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt {5 \times 1 \times 1 \times 3} {/tex}=
= {tex}\sqrt {15} {/tex} sq. cm

questions.question: The edges of a triangular board are 6 cm, 8 cm and 10 cm. The cost of painting it at the rate of 9 paise per {tex}\;c{m^2}{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
s = {tex}{{6 + 8 + 10} \over 2}{/tex}= 12 cm

Area of triangle = {tex}\sqrt {s\left( {s - a} \right)\left( {s - b} \right)\left( {s - c} \right)} {/tex}

= {tex}\sqrt {12\left( {12 - 6} \right)\left( {12 - 8} \right)\left( {12 - 10} \right)} {/tex}

= {tex}\sqrt {12 \times 6 \times 4 \times 2} {/tex}

= 24 sq. cm

Therefore, the cost of painting it at the rate of Rs.0.09 per sq. cm = 24 x 0.09 = Rs.2.16

questions.question: The area of an equilateral triangle of side a is {tex}4\sqrt 3 {/tex} cm2. Its height is given by
questions.explanation:
Area of equilateral triangle = {tex}\frac{\sqrt 3 } {4}{\left( {{\rm{Side}}} \right)^2}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}(\text { Side })^2=4 \sqrt{3}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (Side)2 = 42  
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Side = 4 cm
Area of triangle = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} Base {tex}\times{/tex} Height
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}4\sqrt 3 {/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 4 {tex}\times{/tex} Height 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Height = {tex}2\sqrt 3 {/tex} cm

questions.question: If side of a scalene {tex}\triangle {/tex} is doubled then area would be increased by
questions.explanation:
Area of triangle with sides a, b, c (A) = {tex}\sqrt {s\left( {s - a} \right)\left( {s - b} \right)\left( {s - c} \right)} {/tex}
New sides are 2a, 2b and 2c
Then {tex}s' = {{2a + 2b + 2c} \over 2} = a + b + c{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} s' = 2s .....(i)
New area = {tex}\sqrt {s'\left( {s' - 2a} \right)\left( {s' - 2b} \right)\left( {s' - 2c} \right)} {/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt {2s\left( {2s - 2a} \right)\left( {2s - 2b} \right)\left( {2s - 2c} \right)} {/tex}
= {tex}4\sqrt {s\left( {s - a} \right)\left( {s - b} \right)\left( {s - c} \right)} {/tex}
= 4A
Increased area = 4A - A = 3A
% of increased area = {tex}{{3{\rm{A}}} \over {\rm{A}}} \times 100{/tex} = 300%

questions.question: If each side of a {tex}\triangle {/tex} is halved then its perimeter will be decreased by
questions.explanation:
Perimeter of triangle with sides a, b and c is P = a + b + c. .....(i)
New sides are {tex}{a \over 2},{b \over 2},{c \over 2}{/tex}
New perimeter = {tex}{{a + b + c} \over 2} = {{\rm{P}} \over 2}{/tex}. (From eq.(i))
Decreased perimeter = P - {tex}{{\rm{P}} \over 2}{/tex}= {tex}{{\rm{P}} \over 2}{/tex}
% of decreased perimeter = {tex}{{{\rm{P/2}}} \over {\rm{P}}} \times 100{/tex} = 50%

questions.question: Length of perpendicular drawn on smallest side of scalene triangle is
questions.explanation: Length of the perpendicular drawn on the smallest side of the scalene triangle is largest.

questions.question: Length of perpendicular drawn on longest side of a scale {tex}\triangle {/tex} is
questions.explanation: Length of the perpendicular drawn on the longest side of a scale is{tex}\triangle {/tex} smallest.

questions.question: Semiperimeter of scalene triangle of side k, 2k and 3k is
questions.explanation: Semiperimeter of scalene triangle of side k, 2k and 3k = {tex}{{k + 2k + 3k} \over 2}{/tex} = 3k

questions.question: The sides of a triangle are 5 cm, 12 cm and 13 cm. then its area is
questions.explanation:
s = {tex}{{5 + 12 + 13} \over 2}{/tex}= 15 cm
Area of triangle = {tex}\sqrt {s\left( {s - a} \right)\left( {s - b} \right)\left( {s - c} \right)}{/tex} 
= {tex}\sqrt {15\left( {15 - 5} \right)\left( {15 - 12} \right)\left( {15 - 13} \right)} {/tex} 
= {tex}\sqrt {15 \times 10 \times 3 \times 2} {/tex} 
= 30 sq. cm
= 0.003 sq. m

questions.question: If the sides of a triangle are doubled, then its area
questions.explanation:
Area of triangle with sides a, b and c.
(A) = {tex}\sqrt {s\left( {s - a} \right)\left( {s - b} \right)\left( {s - c} \right)} {/tex} 
New sides are 2a, 2b and 2c 
s' = {tex}{{2a + 2b + 2c} \over 2}{/tex}= a + b + c = 2s .....(i)
New Area = {tex}\sqrt {s'\left( {s' - 2a} \right)\left( {s' - 2b} \right)\left( {s' - 2c} \right)} {/tex} 
= {tex}\sqrt {2s\left( {2s - 2a} \right)\left( {2s - 2b} \right)\left( {2s - 2c} \right)} {/tex} [From eq.(i)]
= {tex}4\sqrt {s\left( {s - a} \right)\left( {s - b} \right)\left( {s - c} \right)} {/tex} 
= 4A
Therefore, the new area will be four times the old area.

questions.question: Area of an equilateral triangle of side 10 cm is :
questions.explanation:
Area of equilateral triangle = {tex}{{\sqrt 3 } \over 4}{\left( {{\rm{Side}}} \right)^2}{/tex} 
= {tex}{{\sqrt 3 } \over 4}{\left( {10} \right)^2}{/tex} 
= {tex}25\sqrt 3 {/tex} sq. cm

questions.question: The length of the sides of a triangle are 5x, 5x and 8x. The area of the triangle is :
questions.explanation:
s = {tex}{{5x + 5x + 8x} \over 2}{/tex} = 9x cm
Area of triangle = {tex}\sqrt {s\left( {s - a} \right)\left( {s - b} \right)\left( {s - c} \right)} {/tex} 
= {tex}\sqrt {9x\left( {9x - 5x} \right)\left( {9x - 5x} \right)\left( {9x - 8x} \right)} {/tex} 
= {tex}\sqrt {9x \times 4x \times 4x \times x} {/tex} 
= 12x2 sq. cm

questions.question: The area of the the triangle having sides 1 m, 2 m and 2 m is:
questions.explanation:
s = {tex}\frac{1 + 2 + 2}{2} = \frac{5}{2}{/tex}m
Area of triangle = {tex}\sqrt {s\left( {s - a} \right)\left( {s - b} \right)\left( {s - c} \right)} {/tex} 
= {tex}=\sqrt{\frac{5}{2}\left(\frac{5}{2}-1\right)\left(\frac{5}{2}-2\right)\left(\frac{5}{2}-2\right)}{/tex} 
= {tex}=\sqrt{\frac{5}{2} \times \frac{3}{2} \times \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{2}}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{\sqrt {15} }{4}{/tex} sq. m

questions.question: The sides of a triangular flower bed are 5 m, 8 m and 11 m. the area of the flower bed is
questions.explanation:
s = {tex}{{5 + 8 + 11} \over 2}{/tex} = 12 m
Area of triangle = {tex}\sqrt {s\left( {s - a} \right)\left( {s - b} \right)\left( {s - c} \right)} {/tex} 
= {tex}\sqrt {12\left( {12 - 5} \right)\left( {12 - 8} \right)\left( {12 - 11} \right)} {/tex} 
= {tex}\sqrt {12 \times 7 \times 4 \times 1} {/tex} 
= {tex}4\sqrt {21} {/tex} sq. m

questions.question: The length of the sides of a triangle are 5 cm, 7 cm and 8 cm. Area of the triangle is :
questions.explanation:
s = {tex}{{5 + 7 + 8} \over 2}{/tex}= 10 cm
Area of triangle = {tex}\sqrt {s\left( {s - a} \right)\left( {s - b} \right)\left( {s - c} \right)} {/tex} 
= {tex}\sqrt {10\left( {10 - 5} \right)\left( {10 - 7} \right)\left( {10 - 8} \right)} {/tex} 
= {tex}\sqrt {10 \times 5 \times 3 \times 2} {/tex} 
= {tex}10\sqrt 3 {/tex} sq. cm

questions.question: Area of an equilateral triangle of side 2 cm is :
questions.explanation:
Area of equilateral triangle = {tex}{{\sqrt 3 } \over 4}{\left( {{\rm{Side}}} \right)^2}{/tex} 
= {tex}{{\sqrt 3 } \over 4}{\left( 2 \right)^2}{/tex} 
= {tex}{\sqrt 3 }{/tex} sq. cm

questions.question: The base of a right triangle is 8 cm and hypotenuse is 10 cm. Its area will be :
questions.explanation:
Perpendicular = {tex}\sqrt {{{10}^2} - {8^2}} = \sqrt {100 - 64} {/tex} = 6 cm
Area of triangle = {tex}{1 \over 2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} Base {tex}\times{/tex} Height
= {tex}{1 \over 2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 6 = 24 sq. cm

questions.question: An isosceles right triangle has area 8{tex}c{m^2}{/tex}. The length of its hypotenuse is :
questions.explanation:
Area of isosceles right triangle = 8 sq. cm
{tex}{1 \over 2}{/tex}{tex}\times{/tex} Base {tex}\times{/tex} Base = 8    [Since in isosceles right triangle, base and perpendicular are same]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (Base)2 = 16
 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Base = 4 cm
Hypotenuse = {tex}\sqrt {{4^2} + {4^2}} = \sqrt {16 + 16} {/tex} = {tex}\sqrt {32} {/tex} cm

questions.question: Each equal side of an isosceles triangle is 13 cm and its base is 24 cm Area of the triangle is :
questions.explanation:
s = {tex}{{13 + 13 + 24} \over 2}{/tex}= 25 cm

Area of triangle = {tex}\sqrt {s\left( {s - a} \right)\left( {s - b} \right)\left( {s - c} \right)} {/tex}

= {tex}\sqrt {25\left( {25 - 13} \right)\left( {25 - 13} \right)\left( {25 - 24} \right)} {/tex}

= {tex}\sqrt {25 \times 12 \times 12 \times 1} {/tex}

= 60 sq. cm

questions.question: The sides of a triangle are in ratio 3 : 4 : 5. If the perimeter of the triangle is 84 cm, then area of the triangle is :
questions.explanation:
Let the sides be 3x, 4x and 5x.
Then according to quesiton, 3x + 4x + 5x = 84
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 12x = 84
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 7
Therefore, the sides are 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 7 = 21, cm, 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 7 = 28 cm and 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 7 = 35 cm
s = {tex}{{21 + 28 + 35} \over 2}{/tex} = 42 cm
Area of triangle = {tex}\sqrt {s\left( {s - a} \right)\left( {s - b} \right)\left( {s - c} \right)} {/tex} 
= {tex}\sqrt {42\left( {42 - 21} \right)\left( {42 - 28} \right)\left( {42 - 35} \right)} {/tex} 
= {tex}\sqrt {42 \times 21 \times 14 \times 7} {/tex} 
= 21 {tex}\times{/tex} 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 294 sq. cm

questions.question: The sides of a triangle are 4 cm, 8 cm and 6 cm. The length of the perpendicular from the opposite vertex to the longest side is :
questions.explanation:
s = {tex}\frac{4 + 8 + 6} {2}{/tex}= 9 cm
Area of triangle = {tex}\sqrt {s\left( {s - a} \right)\left( {s - b} \right)\left( {s - c} \right)} {/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt {9\left( {9 - 4} \right)\left( {9 - 8} \right)\left( {9 - 6} \right)} {/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt {9 \times 5 \times 1 \times 3} {/tex}
= {tex}3\sqrt {15} {/tex} sq. cm
Area of triangle taking base as longest side = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 8 {tex}\times{/tex} h
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times 8 \times h = 3\sqrt {15} {/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}h = \frac{3}{4}\sqrt {15} {/tex} cm

questions.question: Each side of an equilateral triangle is 2x cm. If {tex}x\sqrt 3 = \sqrt {48} ,{/tex} then area of the triangle is :
questions.explanation:
Here , {tex}x\sqrt 3 = \sqrt {48} {/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\sqrt {16} {/tex}  
Side = 2x
Area of equilateral triangle = {tex}{{\sqrt 3 } \over 4}{\left( {{\rm{Side}}} \right)^2}{/tex} 
= {tex}{{\sqrt 3 } \over 4}{\left( {2x} \right)^2}{/tex} 
= {tex}\sqrt 3 {x^2}{/tex} sq. cm
= {tex}\sqrt 3 {\left( {\sqrt {16} } \right)^2} = 16\sqrt 3 {/tex}

questions.question: The sides of a triangle are x, y and z. If x + y = 7 m, y + z = 9 m, and z + x = 8 m, then area of the triangle is :
questions.explanation:
Adding given three equaitons,
2x + 2y + 2z = 24 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x + y + z = 12
Therefore, s = {tex}\frac{12}{2}{/tex} = 6 m
Area of triangle = {tex}\sqrt {s\left( {s - a} \right)\left( {s - b} \right)\left( {s - c} \right)} {/tex} 
= {tex}\sqrt {6\left( {6 - x} \right)\left( {6 - y} \right)\left( {6 - z} \right)} {/tex} 
= {tex}\sqrt {6\left( {12 - 6 - x} \right)\left( {12 - 6 - y} \right)\left( {12 - 6 - z} \right)} {/tex} 
= {tex}\sqrt {6\left( {y + z - 6} \right)\left( {x + z - 6} \right)\left( {x + y - 6} \right)} {/tex} 
= {tex}\sqrt {6\left( {9 - 6} \right)\left( {8 - 6} \right)\left( {7 - 6} \right)} {/tex} 
= {tex}\sqrt {6 \times 3 \times 2 \times 1} {/tex} 
= 6 sq. m

questions.question: If the area of an equilateral triangle is {tex}16\sqrt 3{/tex} cm2 then the perimeter of the triangle is :
questions.explanation:
Area of equilateral triangle = {tex}{{\sqrt 3 } \over 4}{\left( {{\rm{Side}}} \right)^2}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}{{\sqrt 3 } \over 4}{\left( {{\rm{Side}}} \right)^2} = 16\sqrt 3 {/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (Side)2 = 64 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Side = 8 cm
Perimeter of equilateral triangle = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} side = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 8 = 24 cm

questions.question: The base of a right triangle is 8 cm and hypotenuse is 10 cm. Its area will be
questions.explanation:
Perpendicular = {tex}\sqrt {{{10}^2} - {8^2}} = \sqrt {100 - 64} = \sqrt {36} {/tex} = 6 cm
Area of triangle = {tex}{1 \over 2}{/tex}x Base x Perpendicular
= {tex}{1 \over 2}{/tex}{tex}\times{/tex} 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 6
= 24 sq cm

questions.question: The sides of a triangle are 56 cm, 60 cm and 52 cm long. Then the area of the triangle is
questions.explanation:
s = {tex}{{56 + 60 + 52} \over 2} = {{168} \over 2} = 84{/tex}cm

Area of triangle = {tex}\sqrt {s\left( {s - a} \right)\left( {s - b} \right)\left( {s - c} \right)} {/tex}

= {tex}\sqrt {84\left( {84 - 56} \right)\left( {84 - 60} \right)\left( {84 - 52} \right)} {/tex}

= {tex}\sqrt {84 \times 28 \times 24 \times 32} {/tex}

= {tex}\sqrt {12 \times 7 \times 7 \times 4 \times 12 \times 2 \times 16 \times 2} {/tex}

= 12 x 7 x 2 x 2 x 4

= 1344 sq. cm

questions.question: The radius of the circle whose area is equal to the sum of the areas of the two circles of radii 24cm and 7cm is
questions.explanation:
Let the required radius be R.
Then according to the question,
{tex}\pi{/tex}R2 = {tex}\pi r_1^2 + \pi r_2^2{/tex}
= {tex}\pi \left( {r_1^2 + r_2^2} \right){/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} R2 = {tex}r_1^2 + r_2^2{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} R2 = (24)2 + (7)2
= 576 + 49 = 625
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} R = 25 cm

questions.question: The area of a sector of a circle with a sector angle {tex}\theta{/tex} is given by
questions.explanation: The area of a sector of a circle with sector angle {tex}\theta{/tex} is given by{tex}\frac{{\pi {r^2}\theta }}{{{{360}^ \circ }}}{/tex}, where r = radius of the circle

questions.question: The area of the square that can be inscribed in a circle of radius 12cm is
questions.explanation:
According to question, Diameter (BD) = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} radius = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 12 = 24 cm
{tex}\therefore {/tex} Area of square = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times {d^2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times 24 \times 24{/tex} = 288 sq. cm

questions.question: The area of the circle that can be inscribed in a square of side 10 cm is
questions.explanation:
Since if a circle inscribed a square,
then the radius of the circle is half of the side of the square.
{tex}\therefore {/tex} Radius = {tex}\frac{{10}}{2}{/tex} = 5 cm
{tex}\therefore {/tex} Area of the circle = {tex}\pi {r^2}{/tex} = {tex}\pi {\left( 5 \right)^2}{/tex} = {tex}25\pi {/tex} sq. cm

questions.question: A piece of wire 20cm long is bent into the form of an arc of a circle subtending an angle of 60o at its centre. The radius of the circle is
questions.explanation:
Given: Length of arc = 20 cm
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} {tex}\frac{\theta }{{{{360}^ \circ }}}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 2{tex}\pi{/tex}r= 20
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} {tex}\frac{{{{60}^ \circ }}}{{{{360}^ \circ }}}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 2{tex}\pi{/tex}r= 20
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex}{tex}\frac{{\pi r}}{3}{/tex}= 20
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} {tex}r\left( {\frac{\pi }{3}} \right){/tex} = 20
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} {tex}r\left( {\frac{{\pi }}{3}} \right){/tex} = 20
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} r = {tex}\frac{{60}}{{ \pi }}{/tex} cm

questions.question: On a square handkerchief, nine circular designs each of radius 7 cm are made. The area of the remaining portion of the handkerchief is
questions.explanation:
Here Side of square ABCD = AB = 3 {tex}\times {/tex} diameter of circular design
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} AB = {tex}3 \times \left( {2 \times 7} \right) = 42{\text{ }}cm{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {/tex} Area of square = 42 {tex} \times {/tex} 42 = 1764 cm2
And Area of one circular design = {tex}\pi {r^2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{22}}{7} \times 7 \times 7{/tex} = 154 sq. cm
{tex}\therefore {/tex} Area of 9 circular designs = 154​​​​​​​ {tex}\times {/tex} 9 = 1386 sq. cm
{tex}\therefore {/tex} Area of remaining portion of handkerchief= 1764 - 1386 = 378 sq. cm
​​​​​​​​​​​​​​

questions.question: Area of a sector of angle p (in degrees) of a circle with radius R is
questions.explanation:
Area of the sector of angle p of a circle with radius R

{tex}= {\theta \over 360} \times \pi r ^2 = {p \over 360} \times \pi R ^2{/tex}

{tex}= {p \over 2(360)} \times 2 \pi R ^2 = {p \over 720} \times 2\pi R ^2{/tex}

questions.question: If the perimeter of a sector of a circle of radius 6.5 cm is 29 cm, then its area is
questions.explanation:
We know that perimeter of a sector of radius, r {tex}=2 r+\frac{\theta}{360} \times 2 \pi r{/tex} …(1)
Therefore, substituting the corresponding values of perimeter and radius in equation (1), we get,
{tex}29=2 \times 6.5+\frac{\theta}{360} \times 2 \pi \times 6.5{/tex} …(2)
{tex}29=2 \times 6.5\left(1+\frac{\theta}{360} \times \pi\right){/tex}
{tex}\frac{29}{2 \times 6.5}=\left(1+\frac{\theta}{360} \times \pi\right){/tex}
{tex}\frac{29}{2 \times 6.5}-1=\frac{\theta}{360} \times \pi{/tex} ………(3)
We know that area of the sector {tex}=\frac{\theta}{360} \times \pi r^{2}{/tex}
From equation (3), we get
Area of the sector {tex}=\left(\frac{29}{2 \times 6.5}-1\right) r^{2}{/tex}
Substituting r = 6.5 we get,
Area of the sector  {tex}=\left(\frac{29}{2 \times 6.5}-1\right) 6.5^{2}{/tex}
{tex}=\left(\frac{29 \times 6.5^{2}}{2 \times 6.5}-6.5^{2}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\left(\frac{29 \times 6.5}{2}-6.5^{2}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\left(\frac{29 \times 6.5}{2}-6.5^{2}\right){/tex}
{tex}=(94.25-42.25){/tex}
= 52
Therefore, area of the sector is 52 cm2.

questions.question: If the area of a sector of a circle bounded by an arc of length 5{tex}\pi{/tex} cm is equal to 20{tex}\pi{/tex} cm2, then find it's radius
questions.explanation:
We have given length of the arc and area of the sector bounded by that arc and we are asked to find the radius of the circle.
We know that area of the sector {tex}=\frac{\theta}{360} \times \pi r^{2}{/tex}.
Length of the arc {tex}=\frac{\theta}{360} \times 2 \pi r{/tex}
Now we will substitute the values.
Area of the sector {tex}=\frac{\theta}{360} \times \pi r^{2}{/tex}
{tex}20 \pi=\frac{\theta}{360} \times \pi r^{2}{/tex} ……..(1)
Length of the arc {tex}=\frac{\theta}{360} \times 2 \pi r{/tex}
{tex}5 \pi=\frac{\theta}{360} \times 2 \pi r{/tex} ……..(2)
{tex}\frac{20 \pi}{5 \pi}=\frac{\frac{\theta}{360} \times \pi r^{2}}{\frac{\theta}{360} \times 2 \pi r}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{20}{5}=\frac{r^{2}}{2 r}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore 4=\frac{r}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {/tex} r = 8
Therefore, radius of the circle is 8 cm.

questions.question: If AB is a chord of a circle of length {tex}5 \sqrt{3}{/tex} cm with centre O and radius 5 cm, then area of sector OAB is
questions.explanation:
We have to find the area of the sector OAB.

We have,
{tex}\mathrm{AM}=\frac{5 \sqrt{3}}{2}{/tex}
So,
{tex}\sin \angle \mathrm{AOM}=\frac{5 \sqrt{3}}{2(5)}{/tex}
Hence,
{tex}\angle \mathrm{AOM}=60^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle A O B=120^{\circ} {/tex}
Area of sector AOB {tex}=\frac{120}{360} \times \pi \times 5^{2} \mathrm{cm}^{2}=\frac{25 \pi}{3}~ \mathrm{cm}^{2}{/tex}

questions.question: If the area of a sector of a circle is {tex}\frac{5}{18}{/tex} of the area of the circle, then the sector angle is equal to
questions.explanation:
We have given that area of the sector is {tex}\frac{5}{18}{/tex} of the area of the circle.
Therefore, area of the sector {tex}=\frac{5}{18} \times{/tex}area of the circle
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\theta}{360} \times \pi r^{2}=\frac{5}{18} \times \pi r^{2}{/tex}
Now we will simplify the equation as below,
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{\theta}{360}=\frac{5}{18}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \theta=\frac{5}{18} \times 360{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \theta=100{/tex}
Therefore, sector angle is {tex}100^{\circ}{/tex}.
